title: Pre-fetch the embedding model in the installer — offline-first is currently false on first run
rationale: The product's core claim is that it works offline. It does not, on a clean offline install. Every embedder vector_store.py can load is fetched lazily from HuggingFace, and the installer provisions only the GGUF. The failure is already loud and well-documented in code (BL-374, _record_embedder_failure) — what is missing is the 10-line install step that prevents it. Ship potion-base-8M (~30 MB, MIT) into the install payload and point HF_HOME at it. This is not reinvention; it is a missing last mile on the headline feature, and it is the single highest-integrity item in this audit.
evidence: `rg -ni "potion|model2vec|snapshot_download|huggingface|hf_hub|prefetch|MiniLM|nomic"` over install/*.ps1, install/*.py, install.ps1, install.sh, installer/*, scripts/setup_layla.py => ZERO matches. agent/layla/memory/vector_store.py:88-95 documents the breach verbatim: 'There is no pre-fetch in the installer — it provisions the GGUF and nothing else'. model2vec not installed in .venv (find_spec=None).
risk: Adds ~30 MB to the installer payload and one more artifact to version. If the pre-fetch step itself fails on a locked-down box the install should warn, not abort — semantic memory degrading is survivable, a failed install is not.
oss_candidate: minishlab/potion-base-8M via model2vec (MIT, MinishLab, active through 2026). Static embeddings, pure NumPy inference, no torch, ~92% of MiniLM quality. Fully vendorable — weights are a single small file and inference needs no network.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Install model2vec and drop torch from the CPU install path
rationale: The code already prefers model2vec on low tiers, the _Model2VecAdapter is already written and complete, and config_schema.py already exposes embedder_prefer_quality to opt INTO the heavy path. The only thing missing is the package. Installing it lets a 493 MB torch plus 57 MB transformers leave the CPU-only install entirely, on a 4-core/16 GB box where torch buys nothing. This is the rare case where the cheap path is also the better path and the work is already done.
evidence: agent/layla/memory/vector_store.py:112 _Model2VecAdapter (complete, docstring: 'pure numpy, no torch, ~30k sentences/s on CPU, ~92% of MiniLM quality — the potato-tier default'). agent/config_schema.py:21,59 already treat model2vec as the default and sentence-transformers as the opt-in. Measured in .venv: torch 493 MB, transformers 57 MB, sentence_transformers 2 MB. model2vec find_spec=None — declared in [cpu] extra, not installed.
risk: potion-base-8M is 256-dim vs MiniLM 384-dim, so existing vectors must be re-embedded or kept in a separate collection — fallback_store keys the sqlite-vec index on a fixed dim. Re-embedding 590 vectors is seconds, but do not mix dims in one collection. Retrieval quality drops slightly (~8%); keep embedder_prefer_quality as the escape hatch.
oss_candidate: model2vec (MIT, MinishLab, actively maintained 2026). Already declared in the [cpu] extra in pyproject.toml — this is an install fix, not a dependency decision.

challenge.reason:
The evidence checks out but the headline claim does not follow from the proposed action. VERIFIED: `_Model2VecAdapter` is complete at agent/layla/memory/vector_store.py:112 and is genuinely wired — `_get_embedder()` (line ~213) tries `from model2vec import StaticModel` FIRST unless `embedder_prefer_quality`, then falls back to sentence-transformers. `find_spec('model2vec')` is None in C:/Work/Programming/Layla/.venv, so that preferred branch has never executed. Measured on disk in that venv: torch 493.0 MB, transformers 56.8 MB, sentence_transformers 1.9 MB, torchao 5.8 MB.

FALSIFIED — installing model2vec does not remove torch from the CPU install path. pyproject.toml `[cpu]` (lines 68-96) declares model2vec>=0.5 AND `sentence-transformers>=3.0,<4` AND `torchao>=0.6` in the same extra. Both of the latter hard-require torch, so `pip install .[cpu]` installs all 493 MB regardless of whether model2vec is present. Actually dropping torch requires removing two more lines from `[cpu]`, which the recommendation does not propose and which is a separate, larger decision — the recommendation is really "install one package", priced and sold as "delete 550 MB".

Torch also has other live consumers that would go dark: agent/services/prompts/prompt_compressor.py:93 (reachable per-turn via services/agent/reasoning_handler.py:97), agent/services/retrieval/reranker.py:91 and agent/layla/memory/vector_store_rerank.py:33 (CrossEncoder), agent/layla/tools/impl/analysis.py:513-668 (BLIP captioning + transformers pipelines), agent/services/llm/airllm_runner.py. These are try/except-guarded and degrade rather than crash, so removing torch is *survivable* — but it is a capability trade the recommendation never names.

It also does not fix offline/local-first. The BL-374 comment block in the same file states every embedder including potion-base-8M is fetched lazily from HuggingFace with no installer pre-fetch. Swapping MiniLM for potion swaps one un-bundled download for another.

The effort is dishonest at "hours" for the stated goal. `pip install model2vec` alone is minutes; delivering the claimed torch removal is pyproject surgery + verifying five degraded subsystems + a data migration + re-measuring retrieval quality.
challenge.what_it_would_break:
The risk section understates the real failure and gets the migration wrong. Live data measured read-only: agent/layla/memory/chroma_db/fallback_knowledge.sqlite holds 590 vectors and fallback_learnings.sqlite holds 7, ALL at dim 384 (all-MiniLM, not the 768 nomic path). potion-base-8M is 256-dim.

fallback_store.py does not crash on a dim change — it is defensive in a way that makes this WORSE. Line 134 rebuilds the sqlite-vec index at the new dim, and line 333 filters brute-force search to `int(v.shape[0]) == target_dim`, i.e. every one of the 590 existing knowledge vectors is silently SKIPPED at query time. Knowledge/RAG retrieval returns empty with no error, no log, no health signal — this repo's signature failure mode exactly.

And nothing re-embeds them. The only reindex trigger is `refresh_knowledge_if_changed` (vector_store.py:1339), whose fingerprint (`_knowledge_dir_fingerprint`, line 1318) is computed over the knowledge DIRECTORY CONTENTS, not over the embedder model or dim. Swapping embedders does not invalidate it. So the 590 vectors stay stale-dim and invisible until someone happens to edit a knowledge doc. "Re-embedding 590 vectors is seconds" is only true if a re-embed command exists — I found none; the reindex path is doc-change-driven only. Note also sqlite_vec is MISSING from the venv, so the store is running brute-force numpy, which is where the dim filter lives.

Minimum honest scope: (1) `pip install model2vec`; (2) write a one-shot re-embed/migration that rebuilds both fallback stores at the new dim, or make the knowledge fingerprint include `_current_model_name` + `_embedder_dim` so a model swap forces reindex; (3) make a dim mismatch LOUD (log/health) instead of a silent skip — this is worth doing on its own merits regardless of model2vec; (4) only then, separately, consider removing sentence-transformers and torchao from `[cpu]` and accept losing cross-encoder rerank, BLIP captioning, and llmlingua compression on that profile.

title: Route token counting through the Qwen tokenizer already sitting in the GGUF
rationale: Budgeting a Qwen2.5 prompt with OpenAI's cl100k is guessing with extra steps. The exact tokenizer is already on disk inside the GGUF and llama-cpp-python is already installed and already holds a Llama instance at inference time — Llama.tokenize() is exact and costs nothing. Keep tiktoken as the fallback for when no model is loaded. No new dependency, no new concept, and it fixes a real multilingual regression rather than a theoretical one.
evidence: agent/services/llm/token_count.py:17 hardcodes tiktoken.get_encoding('cl100k_base'). I parsed the GGUF header of models/Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf directly (no model load): tokenizer.ggml.model=gpt2, tokenizer.ggml.pre=qwen2, vocab=151,936 vs cl100k 100,277. Measured cl100k-vs-Qwen divergence: English 0%, Python 0%, Spanish +10%, Russian +41%, Japanese +72%, Arabic +94%, Chinese +100%. llama_cpp find_spec=OK in .venv.
risk: Counting now varies by loaded model, so any test asserting a fixed token count for fixed text will need to pin the fallback. Error direction today is over-counting (over-truncation, conservative) — so this is a quality win, not a crash fix, and correcting it will make prompts genuinely longer on CJK/Cyrillic/Arabic. Verify that does not push low-tier budgets past the context window.
oss_candidate: None needed — llama-cpp-python (MIT) is already a dependency and exposes the exact tokenizer. Adopting HF `tokenizers` instead would add a second source of truth for no gain.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Collapse the three divergent SM-2 implementations into one module
rationale: Three hand-rolled copies that return three different answers for the same review is worse than one wrong copy, because the bug fixed in one silently persists in the others — demonstrably what happened here. Consolidate first into a single shared scheduler module with the canonical formula; that alone removes the divergence at near-zero risk. Only then consider py-fsrs, which is a genuinely better algorithm but a behaviour change, not a refactor.
evidence: Three independent _sm2 implementations: agent/layla/tools/impl/memory.py:122, agent/services/infrastructure/german_mode.py:417, agent/services/infrastructure/language_tutor.py:218. Differential run on identical input (ease=2.5, interval=10, reps=5): q=3 -> memory.py (2.36, 25, 6), german (2.36, 24, 6); q=0 -> memory.py ease 2.3, german ease 2.5, canonical SM-2 ease 1.7. memory.py computes the next interval from the pre-update ease — the exact defect german_mode.py:429-431 documents as already fixed.
risk: The three call sites persist to different schemas (learnings review_ease/review_interval_days/review_reps, german_mode ease_factor/interval, lang_card ease/interval_days as REAL). A shared pure function is safe; unifying the STORAGE is a migration and should be a separate step. Correcting the failure branch will lengthen intervals for previously-failed items — acceptable, but it changes user-visible scheduling.
oss_candidate: py-fsrs / `fsrs` on PyPI (MIT, open-spaced-repetition org, FSRS-6, active Jan 2026) — the scheduler Anki ships. Pure Python, no native deps, fully vendorable and offline. Materially better retention-per-review than SM-2, but it needs difficulty/stability/retrievability columns, so treat as a phase-2 upgrade after consolidation, not a drop-in.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Delete the orphan Qdrant adapter and its dead config knob
rationale: A config option that claims to select a vector backend and is read by nothing is worse than no option — it is a documented lie an operator can act on. Nothing imports the module outside its own test, qdrant-client is not installed, and backend selection in vector_store.py is a hardcoded chromadb try/except that never consults vector_backend. This is not the remote/clustering pillar the operator wants wired; it is a redundant third vector store for a 590-vector corpus.
evidence: AST import scan over 522 non-test agent/**.py files: ZERO importers of layla.memory.vector_qdrant; the only importer is agent/tests/test_vector_qdrant.py (13 import sites). qdrant_client find_spec=None. `vector_backend` appears only at runtime_safety.py:955 and config_migrator.py:79 as a default value, plus vector_qdrant.py's own docstring — no reader anywhere selects on it. vector_store.py:310-315,543-548 hardcodes fallback_store-or-chromadb.
risk: Essentially none — 151 lines of module plus 130 lines of test, no production caller. Remove the vector_backend key from both config defaults in the same commit, or config_migrator will keep re-adding a knob that does nothing.

challenge.reason:
The core claim survives adversarial verification. AST import scan over 946 non-build agent/**/*.py files (build/ and .claude/worktrees excluded) found ZERO importers of layla.memory.vector_qdrant outside its own test file; no importlib/__import__ dynamic path exists either. qdrant_client find_spec=None in the app venv. vector_store.py:310-315,543-548 hardcodes fallback_store-or-chromadb and never consults vector_backend. No UI surface (zero hits under agent/ui/). This is not the remote/clustering pillar the operator ruled must be wired — Qdrant is a redundant third vector backend, not clustering/multi-device/phone/skill-packs/Discord.

The recommendation is right but under-scoped in four ways. (1) It claims "no production caller" and stops there, but agent/tests/integration/test_full_pipeline.py:39,59 asserts `"vector_backend" in cfg` and `cfg["vector_backend"] == "chroma"` — a live integration test pins the key and must be edited in the same commit. (2) Deleting from the two defaults dicts is insufficient: agent/runtime_config.json:403 already persists vector_backend, so the key survives forever on the operator's live config. The correct mechanism is the migrator's existing `_DEPRECATED` set (config_migrator.py:30-33), which pops keys on load — the recommendation never names it. (3) The actual user-facing lie is docs/design/02-memory-and-knowledge.md:38,373, which documents the knob to operators as working; that file is absent from the removal list even though it is the strongest support for the rationale. (4) A SECOND independent qdrant surface exists at agent/capabilities/registry.py:49-53, registering an impl at module_path="capabilities.impl.qdrant_vector" — a directory that does not exist — and that registry IS live via routers/workspace.py:75 (exposes per-type counts only, not names). Out of scope for this DELETE, but the commit does not fully de-qdrant the repo and should not claim to.

Considered and rejected: keeping the module as a stub for remote-inference offload, on the theory that a remote vector DB is adjacent to the pillar the operator wants finished. It is a plain client wrapper, recoverable from git history in one command, and a 590-vector corpus never justifies standing up a vector server on a 4-core CPU box.

Effort "minutes" is honest even with the corrected scope: two file deletions, two defaults edits, one _DEPRECATED entry, two test assertions, two doc sections — 20-40 minutes for one maintainer, no design work.
challenge.what_it_would_break:
Nothing in production. Concretely broken by the change as originally scoped: agent/tests/integration/test_full_pipeline.py:39,59 will fail on the missing key unless those two assertions are updated in the same commit — this is the one real trap, since the recommendation asserted no callers and did not flag it. agent/tests/test_vector_qdrant.py:127-128 has the same assertions but is deleted wholesale anyway.

If vector_backend is dropped from the two defaults dicts WITHOUT adding it to _DEPRECATED in config_migrator.py, the key remains in the operator's live agent/runtime_config.json:403 indefinitely — the dead knob outlives the deletion, which is the exact failure the recommendation set out to fix.

Left untouched (correctly, but should be stated rather than implied resolved): the capabilities/registry.py qdrant entry pointing at the nonexistent capabilities.impl.qdrant_vector, its row in docs/CAPABILITIES.md:15, and the stale build/lib/layla/memory/vector_qdrant.py build artifact.

Recovery if wrong: git revert. 281 lines, no schema change, no data migration, no persisted state depends on it.

title: Make the SQLite fallback store the primary and drop chromadb from [core]
rationale: chromadb is in [core] but not installed, and the 'fallback' has quietly been the real store for the entire live corpus. It is the better fit: no C++ toolchain, no server, one file. I measured the actual scale and the actual query cost and there is no performance case for anything else — and sqlite-vec, the acceleration this store optionally wants, is itself brute-force, so it buys SIMD rather than an algorithm and NumPy already goes through BLAS. Promote fallback_store to primary in name and docs, keep chromadb as an opt-in extra. Do not add a vector database.
evidence: chromadb find_spec=None in .venv despite being in [core]. Live corpus counted read-only: fallback_knowledge.sqlite 590 vectors, fallback_learnings.sqlite 7. Measured NumPy brute-force cosine @384-dim: 590 vecs 0.78 ms/query, 5,900 1.51 ms, 59,000 7.24 ms. Web research confirms ANN indexing is unwarranted below ~100K vectors and that sqlite-vec provides brute-force only, no ANN.
risk: Mostly naming, docs and pyproject churn rather than behaviour — the fallback path is already what runs. Anyone with an existing populated chroma_db needs a migration path, and the log line 'chromadb absent — using compiler-free fallback store' should stop framing the normal case as a degradation. Keep sqlite-vec in [cpu] as a free speedup, but do not treat it as required.
oss_candidate: Recommendation is to adopt NOTHING further. sqlite-vec (Apache-2.0/MIT, active) is a reasonable optional accelerant and is already declared; FAISS, LanceDB and Chroma are all unjustified at 590 vectors and each adds native-build or server burden this box cannot absorb.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Install zeroconf to un-block LAN peer discovery
rationale: The operator wants the remote/clustering pillar finished, not deleted. mDNS discovery is already correctly written against the real library — 470 lines, proper ServiceInfo/ServiceBrowser, graceful degradation — and the only reason it does nothing on this box is that a pure-Python dependency sits in the optional [network] extra and was never installed. This is the cheapest possible progress on that pillar: move it into the install path.
evidence: agent/services/cluster/mdns_discovery.py (470 lines) imports zeroconf at line 258 and logs 'mDNS: zeroconf not installed. Run: pip install zeroconf' at line 260. zeroconf find_spec=None in .venv. Declared only in the optional [network] extra in pyproject.toml, which [cpu] does not include. Also imported by agent/main.py and agent/routers/pairing.py.
risk: Installing it makes the box start BROADCASTING a service on the LAN, which is a real privacy posture change for a local-first product — it should be config-gated off by default and opt-in, not enabled by installation. Verify discovery actually moves work before calling the pillar wired; this repo's signature defect is exactly a correct component with nothing consuming it.
oss_candidate: python-zeroconf (LGPL-2.1-or-later, actively maintained, pure Python). Licence is explicitly acceptable under this repo's own gate — scripts/check_copyleft.py:18 permits 'LGPL/MPL only (weak/file-level copyleft)' and blocks only AGPL/GPL/SSPL, which is why Kokoro was rejected and this is not.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Finish the retry_util adoption it was written for, or drop it
rationale: retry_util was added explicitly to give ad-hoc retry loops one home, and then acquired exactly one caller — a textbook instance of this codebase's signature defect, in the very module meant to fix duplication. The library choice is right (tenacity is installed and correct); only the migration never happened. Either finish it or delete it, but a shared helper with one caller is pure overhead. Note the honest caveat: not all nine loops are retries.
evidence: AST call-graph scan over 2,436 .py files: retry_call/resilient invoked from exactly ONE non-test production site, agent/routers/settings.py (plus its own definition and 5 calls in test_retry_util.py). Separate AST scan for for/while loops containing both a Try and a .sleep() found 9 hand-rolled backoff sites: routers/settings.py:350, services/agent/decision_loop.py:64, services/cluster/node_sync.py:577+603, services/infrastructure/agent_task_runner.py:761, services/infrastructure/background_job_worker.py:162, services/llm/llm_gateway.py:1060, services/planning/engine_plans.py:527.
risk: Several of those nine are polling loops (worker drains, node sync) rather than retries, and converting a poll into a bounded retry would change behaviour badly. Inspect each before migrating; expect maybe 4-5 genuine candidates, not 9.
oss_candidate: tenacity (Apache-2.0) is already installed and already wrapped. No new library — this is a migration, not a decision.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Extract one _sse() helper; do NOT adopt sse-starlette
rationale: I expected to find a broken hand-rolled SSE implementation and did not. The framing is correct (data: + blank line, media_type, Cache-Control: no-cache, X-Accel-Buffering: no), and because every payload goes through json.dumps, newlines are escaped and multi-line frame corruption — the classic hand-rolled SSE bug — cannot occur. There is a keepalive pulse already. So the honest recommendation is a local one-line helper to collapse 54 duplicated f-strings, and explicitly NOT a new dependency.
evidence: 54 hand-formatted `data: ` frame sites across 5 files: routers/agent.py (30), routers/openai_compat.py (8), services/llm/inference_router.py (6), routers/settings.py (6), routers/research.py (4). routers/agent.py:961-964 and 1027-1030 set media_type='text/event-stream' with correct headers; :787 reads ui_stream_keepalive_seconds; :877 emits a pulse frame. All payloads are json.dumps(...) so no raw newline can reach the wire.
risk: Low, but it touches the streaming hot path in the chat router — the one surface where a regression is immediately user-visible. Purely mechanical, so do it as one isolated commit with no behaviour change, and keep the existing keepalive rather than replacing it.
oss_candidate: sse-starlette (BSD-3, maintained) is the obvious candidate and I recommend AGAINST it: its main draws are auto-ping and client-disconnect handling, and the ping already exists. It would add a dependency to replace code that is already correct.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Sweep stale local artifacts that corrupt repo-wide scans
rationale: Git hygiene is genuinely good — build/ and dist/ are untracked and .gitignore is thorough, so this costs collaborators nothing. It costs the maintainer and every AI agent working here a great deal, though: build/lib/ is a stale duplicate of the source tree, so any ripgrep or AST sweep silently returns two hits for every real one and can attribute a finding to a months-old copy. My own first call-graph probe returned build/lib and .claude/worktrees hits alongside the real ones.
evidence: Measured on disk: build/ 4,469,116 bytes across 474 files (stale duplicate source tree); .claude/worktrees/ 14,023,912 bytes across 1,504 files; dist/ 1,180,963 bytes; backups/ 25,485,312 bytes across 10 .db files. Plus specific cruft: agent/runtime_config.json.bak-p13, agent/layla/memory/knowledge_graph.graphml.bak, two ZERO-BYTE stray DBs (agent/layla.db, agent/layla/layla.db), and an empty `~` directory at repo root — the residue of an unexpanded tilde in a shell command. `git ls-files` confirms only 1 tracked file under those paths (repo_state/MASTER_ANALYSIS.md, a real doc).
risk: Confirm the two 0-byte DB files are genuinely unused before deleting — a zero-byte SQLite file can be a path a component expects to exist and will recreate, and deleting it is harmless, but a wrong path resolution would be worth knowing about. Keep backups/. Prune worktrees via git, not rm.

challenge.reason:
Two of the delete targets are unsafe and the rationale's core premise is empirically wrong.

(a) LIVE FILE ON THE DELETE LIST. agent/layla/memory/knowledge_graph.graphml.bak is not cruft — it is the knowledge graph's crash-recovery copy. memory_graph.py:115 writes it (shutil.copy2(GRAPH_PATH, GRAPH_PATH.name + '.bak')); lines 43-48 restore from it when the primary graphml is unparseable, logging 'restored knowledge_graph.graphml from last-good backup (%d nodes)'. Caller proven by reading the module, not by text match. Its docstring credits a prior engineering audit. Deleting it silently removes the only recovery path for the graph.

(b) DIRTY ACTIVE WORKTREE COUNTED AS BLOAT. .claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21 (1,504 files / 14MB — the largest item in the sweep) is a live worktree on branch claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a with 20+ uncommitted modified agent/ui/components/*.js files. The rec's own mitigation ('prune via git') does not work: git worktree prune only clears stale admin entries for directories that no longer exist, so it would leave this untouched — pushing a maintainer toward `worktree remove --force` or rm, which destroys the uncommitted work.

(c) PREMISE FALSIFIED. 'Any ripgrep or AST sweep silently returns two hits for every real one' is false for ripgrep. git check-ignore -v confirms build/ (.gitignore:183), dist/ (:182), backups/ (:188) and .claude/ (:143) are all ignored; a bare repo-wide `rg -l world_state` returned 9 files with ZERO from build/ or .claude/. Duplication appears only under --no-ignore, an explicit path arg, or a raw pathlib.rglob walk — which is what the author's own probe almost certainly did. That is a broken probe, fixed by adding an ignore-dir list to the probe, not by deleting 45MB from the working tree.

What is actually true and worth minutes: agent/runtime_config.json.bak-p13 IS genuinely un-ignored (git check-ignore exit 1) and did surface in my bare rg sweep — one real 16KB pollutant, safe to delete or gitignore. And the two zero-byte DBs are worth INVESTIGATING rather than sweeping: db_connection.py:21 resolves four .parent hops to the repo-root layla.db, so no code path should ever create agent/layla.db or agent/layla/layla.db. Their existence is a symptom of a wrong path resolution somewhere — the kind of miswiring that is this codebase's signature defect. Deleting the evidence before understanding it is the wrong move.

Recast as: fix the sweep probe's ignore list, gitignore the one stray .bak-p13, and trace what created the two 0-byte DBs. Leave the graphml backup, the worktree, and backups/ alone.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Deleting knowledge_graph.graphml.bak removes the knowledge graph's only crash-recovery copy — the next corrupt graphml read quarantines the bad file and returns an EMPTY DiGraph instead of restoring ~real nodes, i.e. total loss of graph memory reaching the prompt. title: Split _migrate_impl (897 lines) — but keep the hand-rolled migration runner
rationale: The instinct to reach for Alembic here is wrong. A PRAGMA-style versioned runner over idempotent DDL is the right design for a single-file local SQLite app with one maintainer, and Alembic would add a dependency, an env.py, a revision tree and an offline/online split to solve coordination problems a one-person local-first app does not have. The genuine problem is shape, not approach: one 897-line function holding 37 CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS and 16 ALTER TABLE statements, with only 2 schema_version writes — meaning versioning is nearly vestigial and correctness rests entirely on IF NOT EXISTS idempotence.
evidence: agent/layla/memory/migrations.py is 981 lines; AST function-size analysis shows _migrate_impl at 897 lines (L73), the next largest function being migrate() at 18. Text counts within the file: 37 'CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS', 16 'ALTER TABLE', 2 '_set_schema_version(' calls. _get_schema_version reads a schema_version table with a NEWER-than-build guard at L96.
risk: Highest-risk item here — this runs against the live user DB at startup and a mistake corrupts real data. The IF NOT EXISTS idempotence is load-bearing and easy to break while splitting. Do it incrementally behind a backup, extract per-table functions without changing statement order, and do not attempt to retrofit real numbered revisions in the same pass.
oss_candidate: Considered and REJECTED: Alembic (MIT) and yoyo-migrations (Apache-2.0) are both healthy, but both are built for team/multi-environment coordination and neither is worth its complexity for one local SQLite file maintained by one person.

challenge.reason: Every factual claim verifies (AST: _migrate_impl = 898 lines at L73, next largest migrate() = 19; 37 CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS, 16 ALTER TABLE, 2 _set_schema_version, 48 CREATE INDEX; CODE_SCHEMA_VERSION=1). The OSS reasoning also holds — rejecting Alembic/yoyo for a one-person local SQLite file is correct. What fails is the SIMPLIFY action. The function is long but FLAT: AST shows 46 top-level statements, 36 of them Try wrappers around ALTERs, i.e. straight-line append-only DDL with near-zero cyclomatic complexity. Splitting into ~37 per-table functions replaces one ordered block with 37 call sites in a mandatory order — the reading burden moves rather than drops, and indirection is added. Git history confirms low-churn append-only maintenance (15 commits total via --follow) AND that an extraction pass already happened (e30ca67 'refactor(w1): split data-backfill migrations out of migrations.py'), so what remains is the DDL core deliberately kept together. Decisively, the recommendation itself defers the versioning fix to a later pass, so the payoff is purely line-count in one function — for 'days' of effort, on the self-declared highest-risk item, against the live user DB at startup, in a repo whose real defects are unwired subsystems. Wrong allocation for one maintainer. The genuine defect in this file is silent failure, not shape: migrate() at L69-70 catches Exception and only logs logger.warning('DB migrate failed: %s', e), so a failed migration is swallowed and the app proceeds on a half-built schema — while the downgrade guard 25 lines above correctly calls mark_degraded('database', ...). AST also finds 6 pass-only exception handlers inside _migrate_impl. Fixing the failure path to mark_degraded/raise and auditing those 6 handlers is hours, not days, carries far less risk, and addresses what actually bites the operator. Keep the hand-rolled runner and the Alembic rejection; drop the split.
challenge.what_it_would_break: As proposed, the split risks the load-bearing property that ALTER TABLE statements execute after the CREATE TABLE they depend on, and that the whole sequence stays idempotent under IF NOT EXISTS — reordering during extraction corrupts real user data in layla.db at startup, with no test that would catch a subtle ordering slip. Nothing user-facing is gained in exchange. The corrected STRENGTHEN scope breaks much less: making migrate()'s except branch mark_degraded (or re-raise) turns today's silent half-migrated startup into a visible failure, so an operator on a genuinely broken schema would now see an error at boot instead of confusing missing-column errors later. That is the intended behavior change, but it means any environment currently limping along on a partially-applied schema starts failing loudly — check that the 6 pass-only handlers guard genuinely-optional DDL (already-applied ALTERs) before tightening them, or a benign duplicate-column error becomes a boot failure.

title: Leave the skill sandbox as-is — no OSS option fits this threat model
rationale: I checked this for reinvention and found none worth acting on. skill_sandbox.py does not claim to be a security boundary — it says so in its own docstring — and delivers what it does claim: a per-pack venv, a filtered environment, path confinement, a wall-clock timeout and output truncation. That is an honest, correctly-scoped mitigation. The available upgrades are all worse trades here: RestrictedPython is not a real boundary either, and WASM or container isolation would break arbitrary-Python skill packs and add a heavy runtime to a CPU-only Windows box. Flagging as INVESTIGATE rather than proposing work: the open question is whether the plugin-install consent gate is the actual control, in which case the sandbox is correctly a defence-in-depth layer and nothing here needs doing.
evidence: agent/services/skills/skill_sandbox.py, 268 lines. Docstring L9 'a pack's entry point is an ordinary subprocess running at the...'; L162-163 states plainly 'NOT a security boundary: this is a subprocess at the operator's full privilege with a filtered environment, a timeout and a path-confinement'. Uses venv + subprocess.run with timeout at L127-129 and L206-215. RestrictedPython and wasmtime both find_spec=None (not installed, not declared).
risk: The risk of ACTING is higher than the risk of leaving it: replacing subprocess isolation with a WASM or container runtime would break existing skill packs and add a dependency this hardware cannot carry. The residual risk of not acting is real but is properly owned by the install-time consent gate, not by this module.
oss_candidate: None recommended. RestrictedPython (ZPL-2.1) provides no real isolation for this threat model; wasmtime-py (Apache-2.0) and container isolation are technically stronger but incompatible with arbitrary-Python skill packs on CPU-only Windows with one maintainer.
area: Reinvention audit — hand-rolled code vs. mature OSS, and offline-capability integrity

title: Strip the UTF-8 BOM from routers/agent.py — it blinds the repo's own AST gates to its largest file
rationale: agent/routers/agent.py is the main chat endpoint and the largest router (1629 LOC). It starts with U+FEFF, so ast.parse() on utf-8-decoded text raises SyntaxError. Python's import machinery strips the BOM, so the app runs fine and nothing ever surfaced this — but every AST tool skips the file. The repo's own gates then swallow it silently: check_architecture.py:139 does `except (SyntaxError, UnicodeDecodeError): continue` and check_imports.py:82 does `except SyntaxError: return []`. So the shared_state-importer ceiling check and the import-policy check have never actually examined the biggest consumer of either. Two-part fix: (a) remove the BOM (one byte), (b) make both checkers count parse failures and fail loudly if the count is non-zero. Part (b) matters more than (a) — this is the 'verify the probe' failure mode encoded into CI.
evidence: ast.parse(open(p,encoding='utf-8').read()) over 1001 files -> exactly 2 failures: agent/routers/agent.py and agent/tests/test_phase7_knowledge_loading.py, both 'SyntaxError: invalid non-printable character U+FEFF (line 1)'. Re-running with encoding='utf-8-sig' -> PARSE FAILURES: 0, IMPORT KEYS 3001->3025. Swallow sites read directly: agent/scripts/check_architecture.py:139-141, agent/scripts/check_imports.py:80-83. Proof of impact: before the fix my graph had zero agent.routers.* import keys and wrongly listed routers.learn as orphaned, despite `from .learn import router` at agent/routers/agent.py:67 and router.include_router(_learn_router) at :69.
risk: Removing a BOM changes file hashes and could in theory upset a tool expecting it — nothing here does; Python, ruff and pytest are BOM-agnostic. Adding a loud parse-failure assertion may fail CI immediately if other files are affected; that is the gate working, but budget minutes to triage. Fix the test file's BOM too.
area: Overengineering + consolidation (duplicate owners, oversized subsystems, layered abstractions with one implementation)

title: Delete services/llm/llm_decision.py — 282 LOC strategy hierarchy with zero importers, documented as if it were live
rationale: This module implements a three-strategy pattern (OutlinesStrategy / InstructorStrategy / PlainJsonStrategy) behind an extract_decision() facade. Nothing imports it — not production, not tests. The real decision path is services/agent/llm_decision.py (658 LOC, imported by agent_loop.py:45-52 as _llm_decision_impl). Worse than dead: docs/design/03-llm-and-reasoning.md:423-435 presents the dead module's strategy table as the actual architecture, so anyone reading the design docs to understand decision-making is reading about code that never runs. Its two structured backends (outlines, instructor) are also not installed and not in requirements, so even wiring it up would fall straight through to PlainJsonStrategy. Delete the module and correct that doc section to point at services/agent/llm_decision.py.
evidence: AST import graph (BOM-fixed, 1001 files): services.llm.llm_decision LOC=282 callers=0 prod=0 — zero including tests. Textual cross-check for 'extract_decision|OutlinesStrategy|InstructorStrategy|PlainJsonStrategy' across the repo returns only (a) the module itself, (b) a descriptive comment at services/agent/llm_decision.py:202-203 calling it 'an alternative entry point', (c) docs/design/03-llm-and-reasoning.md:423-435. Contrast: services.agent.llm_decision prod callers=1 (agent.agent_loop). Dep check in .venv: outlines absent, instructor absent.
risk: Very low — no importer exists, so nothing breaks at import time. Only real risk is losing a reference implementation someone meant to return to; git history preserves it. Must update docs/design/03-llm-and-reasoning.md in the same commit or the docs become wrong in the opposite direction.

challenge.reason:
CONCLUSION HOLDS, EVIDENCE PARTLY WRONG, EFFORT UNDERSTATED.

Verified independently: AST scan of 2436 .py files (0 parse failures, utf-8-sig decode) finds ZERO importers of services.llm.llm_decision — prod and test. All llm_decision imports resolve to services.agent.llm_decision (agent/agent_loop.py:45,48,51 + 3 test files). Not dynamically reachable: agent/services/llm/__init__.py is a bare docstring with no re-export, and there are ZERO string literals matching services.llm.llm_decision repo-wide (the importlib usage in the live sibling is _module_installed, a tool-dependency probe). No structural dependency on the file: absent from check_architecture.py's module list, absent from test_dependency_parity.py, no orphan-inventory test. Docs misdirection confirmed at docs/design/03-llm-and-reasoning.md:416-435.

TWO EVIDENCE CLAIMS ARE FALSE:
(1) 'not in requirements' — pyproject.toml:101 declares instructor>=1.0,<2 and :107 declares outlines>=1.0.0,<2.0.0; python_version<'3.13', both in the [llm] extra.
(2) '.venv: instructor absent' — probing C:/Work/Programming/Layla/.venv/Scripts/python.exe: instructor INSTALLED, outlines absent. So 'even wiring it up would fall through to PlainJsonStrategy' is wrong; InstructorStrategy would be reachable.

Drop that argument — the real one is stronger. services/agent/llm_decision.py implements GBNF -> outlines -> instructor -> plain (:545 run_gbnf_agent_decision, :581 run_outlines_agent_decision, :600 import instructor), a strict SUPERSET of the dead module's three strategies. The dead module is a proper subset of the shipping path, missing the native llama.cpp grammar sampler that is now first priority. Delete on redundancy, not on unreachable backends.

EFFORT: minutes->hours. Deletion is minutes; the doc fix is not a pointer swap. The strategy table omits GBNF entirely, so correcting it requires reading the live 658-LOC path and rewriting table + prose. The stale comment at services/agent/llm_decision.py:202-203 (calling the dead module the refactor target and itself 'kept for backward compatibility') must die in the same commit or the next reader re-derives the wrong model.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Nothing at import time or runtime — no importer exists by AST, no dynamic/string reference, no packaging or arch-check dependency. Specifically NOT broken (the usual killer for this repo's callee/caller pattern): the config keys structured_generation_enabled and use_instructor_for_decisions, shipped in runtime_config.example.json:79-80 and defaulted in runtime_safety.py:621-622, are read by the LIVE module at :538-539 — so no user-facing config is orphaned. The outlines/instructor dependencies are likewise used directly by the live path, so deleting this module does not justify dropping them from the [llm] extra. Only real loss is a reference implementation, preserved in git history. Failure mode if done sloppily: deleting the module without rewriting docs/design/03-llm-and-reasoning.md:416-435 and the services/agent/llm_decision.py:202-203 comment leaves docs pointing at a nonexistent file — wrong in the opposite direction.

title: Collapse the two rerankers into one — the dead one is the good one
rationale: There are two independent reranking implementations totalling 391 LOC, duplicating each other function-for-function: both define _bm25_rerank, _tokenize, and _get_cross_encoder. layla/memory/vector_store_rerank.py (200 LOC) is the live one, wired into search_memories_full(use_rerank=True) which the prompt path calls at services/prompts/system_head_builder.py:273. services/retrieval/reranker.py (191 LOC) has NO production caller — yet it is the better implementation for this box: it adds a FlashRank ONNX backend explicitly documented as 'the potato-path default, BL-103, no torch', caches model instances at module level, and is config-driven via reranker_backend. So the CPU-only box runs the torch-dependent reranker while the torch-free one sits unused. Make services/retrieval/reranker.py the single owner, have vector_store_rerank delegate to it (keeping mmr_rerank, which is genuinely unique), and delete the duplicated BM25/tokenize/cross-encoder code.
evidence: services.retrieval.reranker LOC=191 callers=2 prod=0 (both callers are tests). Live path proven: agent/layla/memory/vector_store.py:1057 'use_rerank: bool = True' and :1126 'if use_rerank and results:'; called with use_rerank=True at services/prompts/system_head_builder.py:273 and services/retrieval/__init__.py:89. Duplicate symbols — reranker.py defines _tokenize:145, _bm25_rerank:150, _get_cross_encoder:84; vector_store_rerank.py defines _tokenize:61, _bm25_rerank:66, _get_cross_encoder:43. FlashRank backend exists only in the dead module (reranker.py:51,67).
oss_candidate: FlashRank (Apache-2.0, actively maintained, ONNX cross-encoder, no torch dependency) is ALREADY the intended primary backend inside the dead services/retrieval/reranker.py — not a new dependency to evaluate, the integration is written and just needs a caller. rank_bm25 (Apache-2.0) would replace both hand-rolled _bm25_rerank implementations, but the local BM25 is short and dependency-free; on a 16GB CPU box I would adopt only FlashRank and keep local BM25.
risk: Retrieval quality shifts when the backend changes, and retrieval feeds the system prompt — so a regression is user-visible as worse memory recall rather than an exception. FlashRank downloads a small ONNX model on first use, which must degrade gracefully offline (local-first constraint); the existing _backend_order already falls back to BM25, so verify that path with the model absent. Gate behind the existing reranker_backend config and compare before/after on real queries before switching the default.

title: Extend the existing config guard to the two seams it cannot see — 20 unguarded disagreements, 7 of which silently disable the personality layer on fresh installs
rationale: tests/test_example_config_matches_shipped_defaults.py is genuinely well-built: it pins divergences WITH reasons and fails on new ones AND stale ones. But it compares exactly one pair of sources (runtime_config.example.json vs config_schema.EDITABLE_SCHEMA) and skips auto_tune PROFILE_KEYS. Two seams remain unguarded. SEAM A: the runtime_safety literal defaults dict is what load_config() actually applies, and it disagrees with config_schema on 4 keys — including max_tool_calls (load_config 20, settings UI advertises 5) and deterministic_tool_routes_enabled (True vs False). Since routers/settings.py:411 renders e['default'] to the operator, the UI describes a config the runtime does not use. SEAM B is the damaging one: for keys present in example.json but ABSENT from EDITABLE_SCHEMA, the guard's loop never visits them — 16 disagreements, including seven enable_* flags (cognitive_lens, personality_expression, behavioral_rhythm, ui_reflection, operational_guidance, lens_knowledge, lens_refresh) True in code but False in the example that seeds a fresh install. Also write_file_max_bytes 5000000 vs 500000 — a 10x difference in a safety limit. Extend the guard to iterate the UNION of all three key sets.
evidence: AST extraction of the single 'defaults = {...}' literal in runtime_safety.py (probe asserted exactly 1 match) -> 427 keys; config_schema.EDITABLE_SCHEMA -> 98; example.json -> 529. Seam A (no guard exists): 4 disagreements — deterministic_tool_routes_enabled True/False, max_tool_calls 20/5, elasticsearch_url None/'', remote_api_key None/''. Seam B (guard structurally cannot reach): 16 — the 7 enable_* flags all code=True/example=False, write_file_max_bytes 5000000/500000, response_cache_enabled True/False, max_ram_percent 95/90, inference_backend 'llama_cpp'/'auto', auto_lint_test_fix_ruff_fix False/True, remote_model_name 'llama3.1'/'layla', langfuse_*_key None/'', knowledge_sources []/2-entry list. Seeding path confirmed at agent/install/setup_existing_model.py:14 and scripts/setup_layla.py:146. Scoping check on the LIVE agent/runtime_config.json: all 7 flags are True there, so THIS box is unaffected — exposure is fresh installs only.
risk: Extending the guard fails immediately on all 20 findings, which is the point but means the work is really 'triage 20 config decisions', not 'write a test'. Each needs a judgement: change the example, change the code, or record a reason. The 7 enable_* flags need a product decision — if the example deliberately ships a quiet personality for new users, that is legitimate and should be written down rather than 'fixed'.

title: Delete the tracked runtime_config.json.bak-p13 — a committed backup acting as a fifth config source
rationale: agent/runtime_config.json.bak-p13 is checked into git. A stale, committed snapshot of runtime config sitting next to the real example files is exactly the artifact a future reader (or a future agent reading the repo) mistakes for a maintained source of defaults. The live agent/runtime_config.json is correctly untracked; this .bak is not. Delete it — git history already holds whatever it preserved.
evidence: `git ls-files | rg runtime_config` returns three tracked paths: agent/runtime_config.example.json, agent/runtime_config.minimal.example.json, and agent/runtime_config.json.bak-p13. The operator's live agent/runtime_config.json is correctly absent from that list.
risk: Essentially none. Confirm nothing references the .bak path by name before deleting (a grep for 'bak-p13' takes seconds); recoverable from git regardless.

challenge.reason:
Holds, with a corrected rationale and a required addendum. Verified: (1) the three tracked paths are exactly as claimed and the live agent/runtime_config.json is correctly untracked (.gitignore:33); (2) NO CALLER — every config loader resolves an exact literal path (scripts/setup_layla.py:26, scripts/run_layla.py:26, scripts/model_selector.py:19, launcher.py:67, launcher/layla_launcher.py:73); there is no glob, no runtime_config.* pattern, and no .bak fallback anywhere in the tree; (3) the only textual reference is a historical note at .planning/phases/13-castilla-repair/VERIFICATION.md:38.

The stated rationale is the WEAK argument and is factually off. It is not a stale example file mistaken for defaults — it is a near-byte-identical copy of the operator's LIVE machine config. Full diff vs the live file is four keys (convo_turns 0->6, sandbox_root ~/layla-workspace -> C:/Work/Programming/Layla, tool_first_enforcement_enabled false->true, use_chroma false->true). 437 keys including models_dir="C:\\Work\\Programming\\Layla\\models" and the full secret-shaped surface (discord_bot_token, slack_bot_token, telegram_bot_token, tailscale_auth_key, remote_api_key, langfuse_secret_key). All are null in this snapshot, so there is no live credential leak — but that is luck, not design.

The real argument is release hygiene against a rule this repo already wrote down and has now failed three times. .gitignore:195-213 documents the knowledge_graph.graphml.bak incident ("the rule used to name the .graphml exactly, so the .bak sibling was left untracked but NOT ignored — one `git add -A` from being committed") and repeats it for .planning/audit/.ci-gate-rc.bak ("the operator's config is one `git add` from the repo. Exactly the .graphml.bak lesson above"). agent/tests/test_release_hygiene.py:58-61 asserts that hole stays closed. The runtime_config rules are `agent/runtime_config.json` (exact) and `agent/runtime_config.json.audit-*` — neither carries the trailing `*` the graphml rule learned to use, so .bak-p13 walked through and got committed.

REQUIRED ADDENDUM: deleting the file alone re-arms the trap for the next .bak-p14. Ship the delete with (a) .gitignore changed to `agent/runtime_config.json*` to match the existing `graphml*` pattern, and (b) one assert in agent/tests/test_release_hygiene.py beside the existing .graphml.bak assertion. Still minutes for one maintainer, and it converts a one-off cleanup into a closed hole.

Fully recoverable from git.

title: Drop the unreachable compression/optimization tiers — 217 LOC gated on four packages that are not installed and not installable
rationale: prompt_compressor.py advertises a 4-tier backend ladder (selective-context -> LLMLingua -> LongLLMLingua -> heuristic) and prompt_optimizer.py offers DSPy and guidance backends. All four packages are absent from the app venv AND absent from requirements.txt and pyproject.toml entirely — there is no documented install path that would make them available, so get_available_tier() can only ever return the heuristic tier and the optimizer can only ever take the structural-rewrite path. This is 'layered abstraction with a single implementation' in its purest form: 217 LOC of tier-selection and lazy-loading machinery guarding code that cannot execute. These are also the wrong tools for this box even in principle — LLMLingua and selective-context work by running a SECOND small language model to score token salience, which on a 4-core CPU already spending ~70s per warm turn would cost more than the context it saves. Delete the unreachable branches, keep the heuristic TF-IDF + redundancy-penalty compressor (decent and dependency-free), and make get_info() honest about having one tier.
evidence: Dep probe in C:/Work/Programming/Layla/.venv: llmlingua absent, selective_context absent, dspy absent, guidance absent; tiktoken INSTALLED (so the probe itself works). `rg -in 'llmlingua|selective|dspy|guidance' agent/requirements*.txt pyproject.toml` -> zero matches for all four. AST function-extent measurement: prompt_compressor unreachable fns = 157 LOC (_compress_rag_with_lingua 43, _compress_with_lingua 38, _compress_with_selective_context 30, _load_lingua 22, _lingua_device 9, _selective_context_available 7, _llmlingua_available 6, _lingua_model 2); prompt_optimizer = 60 LOC (_dspy_optimize 36, _guidance_constrain 20, _use_dspy 2, _use_guidance 2). Total 217.
risk: Low mechanically — the code is unreachable, so removal cannot change runtime behaviour where those packages are absent. The real risk is intent: if a GPU box is planned later, deleting the LLMLingua integration discards written work. Mitigation: git history, plus the constraint set (CPU-only, one maintainer) says optimise for the box that exists. If in doubt, delete the DSPy/guidance paths (clearly wrong here) and merely document the LLMLingua tier as unsupported.

title: Remove the stale .claude/worktrees checkout — a second full copy of the repo that corrupts every repo-wide scan
rationale: There is a complete second copy of the source tree at .claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21, a git worktree pinned to an abandoned branch. It is gitignored, so it costs nothing in the repository proper — but it doubles every ripgrep and AST-based analysis run against the working directory, invisibly. In my first import-graph pass it silently inflated every caller count by exactly 2x (system_head_builder reported 12 production callers; the true figure is 6). Any audit, any static check run with a path glob rather than git's file list, and any future agent doing what I just did gets doubled or contradictory answers. Since the brief explicitly forbids git grep for repo-wide scans (tracked-files-only), tooling here MUST walk the filesystem — which makes this duplicate tree an active hazard rather than a passive one. Prune with `git worktree remove` (or `git worktree prune` if the branch is abandoned).
evidence: `git worktree list` shows three worktrees including C:/Work/Programming/Layla/.claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21 at 29c4fdd [claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a]. Size: 14.0 MB across 1504 files. `git check-ignore -v` -> .gitignore:143 '.claude/'. Measured contamination: with the worktree included the graph parsed 1962 .py files and reported services.prompts.system_head_builder prod callers=12 (six real + six .claude.worktrees.* mirrors); excluding it, 1001 files and prod callers=6.
risk: If branch claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a holds unmerged work, removing the worktree makes it less discoverable — check `git log master..claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a` first. The branch ref survives worktree removal, so nothing is actually lost; only the checkout goes.

challenge.reason:
The recommendation is unsafe as written and its premise is overstated.

(1) FATAL — the worktree holds unique uncommitted work. The risk section checked `git log master..claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a` (correctly: 0 commits, 0 vs origin/master, empty diffstat) and concluded "the branch ref survives worktree removal, so nothing is actually lost; only the checkout goes." That checks commits and misses the working tree. `git status --porcelain` inside C:/Work/Programming/Layla/.claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21 returns 24 entries: 22 modified tracked files (21 agent/ui/components/*.js plus agent/ui/sw.js) and 2 untracked new files. `git diff --stat` = 22 files, 428 insertions, 1 deletion. The change is a coherent, commented feature — BL-386, document-level capture listener so Escape dismisses overlays when focus is on <body>, plus making the advertised "esc" chip actually clickable/keyboard-operable. Its tests are new and untracked: agent/tests/test_overlay_escape.py (45 lines) and agent/ui/tools/test_overlay_escape.mjs (203 lines). None of it exists in master: both test paths are absent from C:/Work/Programming/Layla, and `grep -c "escapeHtml\|escapeAttr" agent/ui/components/kb.js` = 0 in master. `git worktree remove` will refuse on a dirty tree — which is exactly when an operator following this recommendation reaches for `--force` or `rm -rf` + `git worktree prune`, and 428 lines that exist in no commit anywhere are gone.

(2) The contamination premise does not survive measurement. .gitignore:143 is `.claude/`, and .claude is also a dot-directory, so ripgrep excludes it twice over by default: `rg --files --no-ignore -g '*.py' | grep -c .claude` = 0; you must pass BOTH `--hidden --no-ignore` to surface it (then 961). So "doubles every ripgrep run" is false for default rg. For filesystem walkers that ignore VCS metadata, the worktree is not the dominant polluter: `pathlib.Path('.').rglob('*.py')` yields 31,982 files — 16,329 in .venv, 13,217 in .venv-test, 961 in .claude, against only 946 real files in agent/ and 474 in build/. A probe naive enough to swallow the worktree is already 30x more corrupted by the two virtualenvs, which are not deletable. The reported 12-vs-6 caller inflation for services.prompts.system_head_builder is real, but it is a symptom of an import-graph tool with no exclusion list, not of the worktree's existence.

(3) Therefore the fix is not DELETE. Root cause is a missing exclusion list; the durable fix is to exclude `.claude/`, `.venv*/`, and `build/` in the analysis tooling (or add a .rgignore / use rg's defaults), which is minutes and also fixes the far larger venv problem. Before that, the stranded BL-386 work needs an operator decision — review, run the two new tests, commit onto a branch, or consciously discard. Removing the checkout is a trivial afterthought once the work is rescued, not the headline action.

One secondary note the recommendation missed: branch `claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` (bd402fb) exists with no worktree attached, despite the directory being named for it — the checkout is actually pinned to `claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a` (29c4fdd). Worth resolving in the same investigation.

This work exists only in that worktree's working directory — it is in no commit on any branch, local or remote — so a forced worktree removal destroys it irrecoverably, with no reflog and no dangling object to recover from.

title: Adopt retry_util in the genuine I/O retry sites — but do NOT collapse the LLM reprompt loops into it
rationale: services/infrastructure/retry_util.py was written explicitly so that 'ad-hoc `for attempt in range(...)` loops can standardise on it' (its own docstring), backed by tenacity with exponential backoff and jitter plus a stdlib fallback. It has ONE production caller, while eight hand-rolled attempt loops exist — the signature defect again, a consolidation point built and never adopted. HOWEVER, I would push back on a blanket merge: most of those eight are not I/O retries. Loops like services/agent/llm_decision.py:633 and services/planning/planner.py:530 are LLM REPROMPT loops — they retry because the model returned unparseable JSON, and typically need to mutate the prompt between attempts (tighten the grammar, restate the schema). Forcing those through a fn()-with-backoff contract would be a worse design and would add pointless sleep latency to an already slow CPU turn. Adopt retry_util only where failure is genuinely transient I/O. If the count of true I/O sites turns out to be 2-3, consider instead DELETING retry_util and its tenacity dependency — the more honest consolidation.
evidence: services.infrastructure.retry_util LOC=72 callers=2 prod=1. Eight hand-rolled attempt loops located by pattern 'for (attempt|retry) in range(': agent/autonomous/planner.py:73, agent/download_docs.py:99, services/planning/planner.py:530, services/llm/llm_gateway.py:1060, services/agent/llm_decision.py:633, services/planning/coordinator.py:303, services/llm/llm_decision.py:186 (in the dead module — disappears if that is deleted), services/llm/litellm_gateway.py:194. retry_util docstring states the standardisation intent verbatim.
risk: Low per-site, but each conversion must preserve the existing exception filter — retry_call defaults to exceptions=(Exception,), broader than most of these loops, and would start retrying genuine logic errors while adding backoff delay to a fast failure. Convert one site, confirm behaviour, then proceed. Do not batch-convert.

title: Make config_schema the single source of default VALUES; leave runtime_safety owning only hardware-derived overrides
rationale: This is the structural fix behind the guard-extension item, and the largest genuine consolidation in my area. Today runtime_safety.load_config() carries a 427-key literal defaults dict, config_schema.EDITABLE_SCHEMA carries 98 defaults with types/min/max/hints, and the two overlap on 79 keys and disagree on 4. The schema already knows MORE than the dict (types, ranges, UI hints) and load_config already imports it for clamping. Natural end state: config_schema declares every key's default, load_config builds its baseline FROM the schema, and runtime_safety keeps only what is genuinely dynamic — _hardware_derived_defaults() and the lite-mode probe. runtime_config.example.json then becomes a GENERATED artifact rather than a hand-maintained 529-key third opinion, structurally eliminating the drift class the guard currently polices by hand. Sequence AFTER the guard extension, so the guard is watching while you move values.
evidence: runtime_safety.py: single literal 'defaults = {...}' of 427 keys inside load_config (probe asserted exactly one such assignment), followed at :1019 by 'hw_defaults = _hardware_derived_defaults(); defaults.update(hw_defaults)' and at :1027 'defaults.update(data)' from runtime_config.json. config_schema.EDITABLE_SCHEMA = 98 entries carrying default/type/min/max/hint. Overlap 79, disagreements 4. Existing coupling already present at runtime_safety.py:1035-1040 (imports _SCHEMA_BY_KEY + coerce_and_clamp). 19 keys live in the schema but not the runtime dict.
risk: Highest-risk item here and the one to sequence last. load_config is on the startup path for everything; a key that silently changes default value can alter model parameters, safety limits, or feature gates with no error. The 4 known disagreements must each be resolved by decision (which value is correct?) BEFORE the merge, not during it. Mitigate by landing the extended guard first to pin current behaviour, then moving keys in small batches with the suite green between batches. Do NOT attempt as one commit.

challenge.reason:
The diagnosis and nearly all numbers verify, but the prescribed end state misreads what config_schema IS, and inverts the direction of authority.

VERIFIED BY AST: exactly one `defaults = {...}` literal (runtime_safety.py:496, 427 constant keys, 0 non-constant); EDITABLE_SCHEMA = 98 entries (a LIST at config_schema.py:41, not a dict — my first probe asserted a dict and failed loudly); overlap 79; schema-only 19; runtime-only 348. All as claimed.

CORRECTIONS: (1) Disagreements are 6, not 4: deterministic_tool_routes_enabled, enable_self_reflection, max_tool_calls (5 vs 20), tool_call_timeout_seconds (60 vs 180), plus elasticsearch_url and remote_api_key ('' vs None, cosmetic). (2) Only 89 of 98 schema entries carry a `default` at all, so the schema does not currently "know more" about every key.

WHY IT FAILS: EDITABLE_SCHEMA is primarily a WRITE ALLOWLIST enforced at the API boundary, not a defaults table. route_helpers.py:280 `sync_save_settings` computes `rejected = sorted(set(k for k in body if k not in editable) | set(refused_remote))` — keys absent from the schema are refused by POST /settings. It also drives the settings UI via get_schema_for_api (routers/settings.py:438). The 79-key overlap is therefore not accidental duplication; it is exactly the set that is BOTH operator-editable AND code-defaulted. Making config_schema "declare every key's default" either exposes 348 internal knobs as remotely-writable settings, or requires a second structure inside config_schema — relocation, not consolidation, with no net reduction in sources.

AUTHORITY IS INVERTED: at runtime_safety.py:1035-1038 the clamp loop runs only `if _k in data` — only for keys the operator's file already sets. A fresh install with no runtime_config.json NEVER consults a schema default. The runtime dict is authoritative for values today; schema `default` is a clamp fallback plus UI hint. So the proposed direction (schema owns values) is the reverse of the working design.

DRIFT CLASS IS ALREADY GATED: the claim that runtime_config.example.json drift is "policed by hand" is wrong. agent/tests/test_example_config_matches_shipped_defaults.py is an existing automated gate with documented ALLOWED_DIVERGENCES plus staleness and value-drift checks. It covers example-vs-schema; the genuine uncovered gap is schema-vs-runtime.

REAL BUG SURFACED: that test justifies a divergence as "The schema default stays False so the feature is opt-in for anyone assembling a config from scratch" — but the runtime dict ships True for deterministic_tool_routes_enabled, so a from-scratch install gets True. The recorded rationale is defeated by the merge order. Worth fixing on its own.

CORRECTED ACTION (SIMPLIFY, days): keep the runtime dict authoritative for VALUES; strip the duplicate `default` from the 79 overlapping schema entries or pin them equal with a new schema-vs-runtime test; resolve the 6 disagreements by explicit decision. Never touches the allowlist boundary, and is one maintainer's week at most rather than a weeks-long migration of the startup path.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Executed as written, moving the 427 runtime keys under config_schema's ownership would add the 348 runtime-only keys to the EDITABLE_SCHEMA write allowlist, making security-critical config remotely writable via POST /settings: autonomous_allow_network (False), autonomous_tool_allowlist, shell_allowlist_extra, remote_allow_endpoints, remote_cors_origins, remote_require_auth_always, sandbox_python_memory_limit_mb, sandbox_python_timeout_seconds, sandbox_runner_timeout_seconds, plus firecrawl/qdrant/meilisearch/mem0/langfuse API-key fields. It would simultaneously render ~350 internal knobs in the settings UI via get_schema_for_api, since EDITABLE_SCHEMA entries carry category/hint and are enumerated for the UI. Avoiding both requires a hidden/internal flag and a bifurcated schema — at which point two structures still exist and the consolidation premise is gone. Separately, because schema defaults are only consulted through coerce_and_clamp for keys already present in the operator's config file, rebasing load_config onto schema defaults would silently change effective values for any key where the two disagree (6 known today, including max_tool_calls 20->5 and tool_call_timeout_seconds 180->60, both of which directly alter agent turn behaviour), on the startup path, with no error raised.
