title: Collapse the PromptBuilder facade — a class whose own docstring says it exists for tests
rationale: services/prompts/prompt_builder.py defines 'class PromptBuilder' documented verbatim as 'Facade for tests and future callers.' An abstraction that admits in its docstring that it has no production caller is the single-implementation-wrapper pattern stated outright. The module's real work is its module-level functions (build_core_sys_parts, tool_names_for_decision, build_decision_tool_hints, _capability_manifest_core), which both production consumers import directly. If the class exists only so tests have something to instantiate, the tests should call the functions and the class should go. Small, but a load-bearing clarity fix in the prompt-assembly area where LOC is heaviest and confusion is most expensive.
evidence: agent/services/prompts/prompt_builder.py — 'class PromptBuilder' with docstring 'Facade for tests and future callers.', methods build_static and tool_injection. Module LOC=537, production callers=2 (services.agent.llm_decision, services.prompts.system_head_builder), both importing module-level functions rather than the class.
risk: Low, but I did not enumerate which tests instantiate PromptBuilder, so true cost is 'however many test files need their construction line changed'. Check that count first; if large, the churn may outweigh the clarity gain, in which case just fix the docstring to state the truth.
area: Overengineering + consolidation (duplicate owners, oversized subsystems, layered abstractions with one implementation)

title: Determine whether the capability manifest re-states what tool signatures already provide
rationale: I was pointed at this as a known duplication (a hand-maintained capability manifest restating what @functools.wraps tool signatures already expose) and I could not confirm or refute it in this pass, so I report it as an open question rather than guessing. What I did establish: the manifest text reaching the prompt is assembled by _capability_manifest_core(root) in prompt_builder.py, which reads a .identity/capabilities.md file — a hand-written document, therefore a second description of the tool surface alongside the 200 registered tools' own signatures and docstrings. Separately, agent/capabilities/registry.py (255 LOC) is a DIFFERENT thing — an optional-ML-backend registry (reranker, cohere_rerank) — and should not be conflated with the manifest despite the shared word. One number deserving explanation either way: tests/test_capability_manifest.py is 1197 LOC against a 255-LOC registry and a markdown file. The investigation: does .identity/capabilities.md restate tool names/descriptions that layla/tools/domains/*.py TOOLS dicts already declare? If yes, generate the manifest from the registry and delete the hand-maintained copy — a hand-written capability list is exactly the artifact that goes stale and makes the model confidently describe abilities it does not have.
evidence: agent/services/prompts/prompt_builder.py _capability_manifest_core(root) documented as 'The PROMPT-CORE block of .identity/capabilities.md - the verified capability manifest', plus sibling gates _is_capability_question / _is_identity_question. agent/capabilities/ totals 272 LOC (registry.py 255, __init__.py 17) and is an optional-backend registry — registry.py:72-84 registers 'reranker' with module_path 'capabilities.impl.cohere_rerank'. agent/tests/test_capability_manifest.py = 1197 LOC. Tool metadata declared separately in layla/tools/domains/*.py TOOLS dicts, merged by layla/tools/registry.py:22-40.
risk: Investigation only — no change, no risk. Acting on the outcome carries real risk: prior work established that the manifest reaching the model was already a source of confident invention, so any regeneration must be verified accurate BEFORE it is wired in. Fix content before exposure.
area: Overengineering + consolidation (duplicate owners, oversized subsystems, layered abstractions with one implementation)

title: The application has no log file — add a RotatingFileHandler
rationale: Every diagnostic this system produces goes to the stderr of a console window that closes. There is no post-hoc record of any failure, ever. For a one-maintainer local-first app started from START.bat, this is the difference between 'Layla went weird last Tuesday' being diagnosable or not. It is also the precondition for every other finding here: promoting log levels is pointless if nothing persists the output. Stdlib logging.handlers.RotatingFileHandler needs no new dependency, no network, and honours the existing formatter — add it alongside the StreamHandler at agent/main.py:123/132 with maxBytes/backupCount so it stays bounded (the repo already proves it can rotate: db_backup.py keeps 7).
evidence: `grep -rn FileHandler --include=*.py agent/ --exclude-dir=tests` returns ZERO matches. agent/main.py:123 and :132 both execute `_logging.getLogger().handlers = [h]` where h is a single StreamHandler — this REPLACES all handlers, so no other module can add a sink. `ls -la logs/` = one file, audit.log, 920 bytes, mtime 2026-05-20 (two months stale). Default level agent/main.py:100: `os.getenv('LOG_LEVEL','INFO')`.
risk: Very low. Additive — the existing StreamHandler behaviour is unchanged. Only real risk is unbounded disk if maxBytes/backupCount are omitted; set them explicitly (e.g. 5 MB x 3). Ensure the log path honours LAYLA_DATA_DIR like _default_db_path() does, or a packaged install writes into its install dir.
area: UNDERDESIGN — error handling, atomicity, growth bounds, concurrency, and user-visible failure reporting

title: 632 broad-except handlers log only at DEBUG while the default level is INFO — every one is invisible
rationale: This is not a style issue, it is the observability hole that lets 'built well and never plugged in' defects survive for weeks. A handler that reports at DEBUG under a default-INFO root logger is functionally identical to `except: pass`. Do not bulk-promote all 632 — that would drown the console. Triage by blast radius and promote only the handlers on paths where a failure changes what the MODEL SEES or what gets PERSISTED: system_head_builder.py (49), memory_router.py (21), vector_store.py (19), run_setup.py (19), llm_decision.py (16), turn_commit.py (14). Those six files are ~138 handlers and cover prompt assembly, retrieval, and turn persistence. WARNING is the right level for 'a prompt section was dropped'.
evidence: AST classification of broad-except handlers by first log call, non-test, excluding re-raisers: debug=632, warning=291, exception=42, error=37, critical=3, info=2. Top file agent/services/prompts/system_head_builder.py = 49 debug-only handlers in 1652 lines; confirmed samples at :172 `logger.debug('load_learnings failed')`, :289 'mem0 recall merge skipped', :580 'context[knowledge_refresh] failed', :588 'context[project_domains_knowledge] failed'. Each drops a system-prompt section silently. Default root level INFO per agent/main.py:100.
risk: Log noise if over-applied — a handler on a genuinely optional, frequently-absent path (e.g. an unconfigured integration) will now warn every turn and train the operator to ignore warnings. Mitigate by promoting file-by-file and watching one real session's output before moving to the next file. Use warning-once/rate-limited logging for per-turn paths.
area: UNDERDESIGN — error handling, atomicity, growth bounds, concurrency, and user-visible failure reporting

title: Enable ruff BLE001 + S110/S112 — the linter is already installed and the rules are simply unselected
rationale: The repo already runs ruff in CI and has a populated .ruff_cache. The rules that detect exactly the 1620-handler problem — BLE001 blind-except, S110 try-except-pass, S112 try-except-continue — are not in the select list. This is the cheapest possible way to stop the population GROWING, which matters more than retiring the existing 1620. Scope it honestly: enable the rules, generate a baseline via per-file-ignores for the existing offenders, and let CI block only NEW ones. That converts an open-ended cleanup into a ratchet a single maintainer can actually hold. Do NOT attempt a repo-wide fix — many of these swallows are deliberate best-effort paths.
evidence: pyproject.toml:214 `select = ["E", "F", "W", "I"]` — no BLE, no S. pyproject.toml:222 already demonstrates the per-file-ignores baseline pattern. Population to baseline: 1620 silent swallows in non-test code (617 pass, 626 return, 347 log-less, 30 continue) out of 3479 total handlers = 47%. Top files: routers/system.py 54, layla/tools/impl/file_ops.py 43, layla/tools/impl/general.py 36, main.py 33, layla/memory/learnings.py 33.
risk: Generating the baseline is mechanical but the per-file-ignores block will be large (~100 files) and will look alarming in pyproject.toml. It can also mask a file entirely — if a file is baselined, new blind-excepts in THAT file are not caught. Accept that; the alternative is no ratchet at all. S110 in particular has false positives on legitimate cleanup paths (e.g. the `try: existing.close() except: pass` in db_connection.py:79-81, which is correct).
area: UNDERDESIGN — error handling, atomicity, growth bounds, concurrency, and user-visible failure reporting

title: The sqlite-vec index permanently self-disables on any single exception, with zero logging
rationale: `_vec_ok` is set True exactly once (at init, line 74). Six separate code paths set it False, each inside a bare `except Exception:` with NO log call whatsoever, and nothing ever sets it back. One transient error — a locked DB, a malformed vector, a dimension mismatch during an embedder swap — permanently downgrades semantic recall to brute-force for the entire process lifetime. Search correctness survives (line 303 falls through to brute force), so this is degradation not corruption, which is precisely why it would never be noticed: memory just gets quietly worse and slower. The comment 'never let the index break a write' is the right INTENT; the missing piece is that intent was implemented as silence rather than as a logged, recoverable degradation. Add a warning-once log at each transition and a reset path on reopen.
evidence: agent/layla/memory/fallback_store.py — `_vec_ok = True` appears once, at :74. Set False at :67, :76, :86, :151 (`self._vec_ok = False  # never let the index break a write`), :159, :307 (`# fall through to brute force on any error`). No logger call in any of those handlers. agent/layla/memory/vector_store.py separately carries 19 debug-only broad-except handlers.
risk: Low. Adding a log is inert. A reset/retry path is slightly more invasive — if the underlying cause is persistent (e.g. sqlite_vec extension genuinely unavailable) a naive retry would log on every write; gate the retry on reopen or on a time interval, not on every call.
area: UNDERDESIGN — error handling, atomicity, growth bounds, concurrency, and user-visible failure reporting

title: Derived-memory writes run on unjoined daemon threads — silently dropped at shutdown, no retry, no record
rationale: commit_turn fires learning extraction, entity extraction, skill acquisition, title synthesis, and capability practice onto daemon=True threads that nothing joins. Daemon threads are killed instantly at interpreter exit. If the operator closes the window shortly after a turn — the common case on a laptop — that turn's derived memory is lost with no error, no queue entry, and no way to know it happened. The core message write is synchronous so the CONVERSATION is safe; what is lost is everything that makes the conversation useful later. The cheap fix is not to make them synchronous (that would put LLM calls on the response path) but to register the threads and join them with a short timeout in the existing shutdown hook, which already calls close_thread_connection() at agent/main.py:671.
evidence: agent/services/agent/turn_commit.py: :271 name='title-synth' daemon=True, :428 name='skill-acquire' daemon=True, :469 name='auto-learn' daemon=True, :491 name='cap-practice' daemon=True — plus extract_in_background at :491. All wrapped in handlers that report at logger.debug ('commit_turn: skill acquisition failed', 'commit_turn: learning extraction failed'), i.e. invisible at default INFO. 37 threading.Thread constructions in non-test code; a shutdown hook already exists (agent/main.py:671 close_thread_connection).
risk: A join-with-timeout at shutdown makes exit slower and, if a background task hangs on an LLM call, the timeout is what saves you — pick it deliberately (2-3s) and keep daemon=True so a hung thread can still never block exit permanently. Do not convert these to synchronous calls; that would regress response latency badly on this CPU-only box.
area: UNDERDESIGN — error handling, atomicity, growth bounds, concurrency, and user-visible failure reporting

title: Confirm whether rl_preferences / entities / tasks need retention, or are deliberately permanent
rationale: 30 live tables have no retention policy. I want to be explicit that MOST of these are correctly excluded — learnings, entities, user_identity, capabilities, goals, project_context are the operator's actual memory and auto-deleting them would be a bug, not a fix. The genuinely open question is the few that accumulate mechanically rather than meaningfully: rl_preferences (156 rows, no time column at all, so age-based retention is not even expressible) and capability_dependencies/style_profile. At 4.08 MB total after months of use this is NOT urgent and I will not dress it up as such — it is a 'decide the policy before it matters' item. The missing time column on rl_preferences is the actionable part: without it, no retention policy can ever be written for that table.
evidence: Diff of 63 live tables against 22 parsed policy entries in agent/services/memory/memory_consolidation.py. Uncovered with rows>0: rl_preferences 156 (no created_at/ts), tasks 96 (has time col), entities 86 (has), user_identity 40 (no), learnings 35 (has), capability_domains 23, capabilities 23 (no), conversations 19, capability_dependencies 12 (no), style_profile 4 (no). Live DB total 4.08 MB, WAL 0.0 MB. Retention job IS wired: apply_retention_policies called at agent/layla/scheduler/jobs.py:195 (1 non-test callsite, AST-verified).
risk: The real risk here is OVER-correcting: adding retention to a table that holds durable operator memory would destroy data that has no backup beyond the 7 rotating snapshots. Any new policy must be argued per-table, and learnings/entities/user_identity/goals should stay excluded. Adding a created_at column to rl_preferences is a migration — cheap, but it is a schema change on the live DB.
area: UNDERDESIGN — error handling, atomicity, growth bounds, concurrency, and user-visible failure reporting

title: Delete logs/audit.log — a tracked orphan no code writes to
rationale: logs/audit.log is tracked despite BOTH `*.log` and `logs/` being in .gitignore (tracked files override ignore rules). It holds 4 JSON lines from 2026-02-19 and has not changed since. I verified no code targets it: ripgrep for `"logs/`, `Path("logs"`, and `/ "logs"` across all Python source returns empty. The real audit-log writer resolves to `agent/.governance/audit.log` (agent/main.py:776 `AUDIT_LOG = GOV_PATH / "audit.log"`, agent/layla/scheduler/jobs.py:21, agent/layla/tools/impl/general.py:684), which IS correctly gitignored. title: Delete agent/scripts/last_report.json — committed runtime snapshot whose stale numbers are cited as fact
rationale: This is the exact 'runtime snapshot committed by accident' class the operator asked about. It is generated output: `agent/scripts/run_all_checks.py:318` does `report_path = SCRIPTS_DIR / "last_report.json"` and overwrites it on every run, so it dirties the working tree after any check run. It is frozen at timestamp 2026-05-13 (over two months stale). The real damage is downstream: agent/docs/audit/subsystem_audit.md quotes its stale fields as present-tense truth in six places, including 'last_report.json:7 says repo_index_populated=false' and 'confidence_pct: 100' — while the actual committed file says confidence_pct 91 and repo_index_populated TRUE. The doc and the artifact it cites have already drifted apart, so the file now actively generates wrong conclusions. Deleting it and letting each run regenerate locally removes the false authority.
evidence: agent/scripts/last_report.json, 2580 bytes, tracked. Content: `"timestamp": "2026-05-13T10:35:52Z", "confidence_pct": 91, "repo_index_populated": true`. Writer: agent/scripts/run_all_checks.py:318. Documented as output in agent/scripts/README.md:46 and docs/design/11-installation-deployment.md:556. Contradicting citations in agent/docs/audit/subsystem_audit.md lines 19, 30, 89, 158, 177, 194.
risk: Low. Any consumer regenerates it by running run_all_checks.py. Commands: `git rm agent/scripts/last_report.json` then add `agent/scripts/last_report.json` to .gitignore (it is NOT currently ignored, so it silently returns on the next `git add -A` otherwise). Separately worth fixing the six stale citations in agent/docs/audit/subsystem_audit.md.

challenge.reason: Every load-bearing claim verified. Writer confirmed at agent/scripts/run_all_checks.py:318 (`report_path = SCRIPTS_DIR / \"last_report.json\"`), overwritten unconditionally on every run. Readers: ZERO — ripgrep for both `last_report` and `confidence_pct` across .py/.js/.html/.yml/.ps1/.sh/.toml returns only the writer's own lines (7, 310, 318); no module names the file, so no AST caller is possible. Size/content exact: 2580 bytes, timestamp 2026-05-13T10:35:52Z (~2.3 months stale vs today 2026-07-22), confidence_pct 91, repo_index_populated true. It is the ONLY committed generated artifact under agent/scripts/ (`git ls-files agent/scripts/ | rg '\\.json$'` returns it alone), so removal makes the directory consistent rather than creating an exception. The documented drift is real and WORSE than the recommendation states: subsystem_audit.md:19 asserts confidence_pct 100 with real_assertions_pass 1/3 while the file says 91 with 3/3; line 203 builds a 1-hour remediation item on `config_cache_importable=false` when the file says true; line 177 cites 854 tests passed when the file says 1445. The audit doc is generating fabricated work items from an artifact that already contradicts it — exactly the \"runtime snapshot cited as fact\" class. Nothing here touches the clustering/multi-device/phone/skill-packs/Discord pillar, and no working hand-rolled code is traded for a dependency.

title: Remove the stale 17 MiB git worktree and two merged branches — but NOT the branch with 2 unmerged commits
rationale: `.claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` is 17 MiB of duplicated repo checkout, untouched since Jul 18. It is a REGISTERED git worktree (it appears in `git worktree list`), so `rm -rf` would leave a broken registration and a stale entry in .git/worktrees — the correct command is `git worktree remove`. The naming is a trap worth spelling out: the DIRECTORY is named mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21 but the branch checked out inside it is `claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a`, which has 0 commits not in master. The similarly-named BRANCH `claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` has no worktree and DOES carry 2 commits not in master. Deleting by name-matching would destroy unmerged work. Also merged and safe to prune: local branch `integration` (0 unmerged), and remotes origin/friend-ready-session and origin/unite-integration (0 unmerged each).
evidence: `du -sh .claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` -> 17M, mtime Jul 18 03:47. `git worktree list` -> that path is on branch [claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a] at 29c4fdd. `git log --oneline master..claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a` -> 0 commits. `master..claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` -> 2 commits. `master..integration` -> 0. `master..origin/friend-ready-session` -> 0. `master..origin/unite-integration` -> 0. `.claude/` is gitignored so none of this is tracked.
risk: MEDIUM if done carelessly, LOW in this order. Safe sequence: `git worktree remove .claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` (add --force only if it reports dirty state, and inspect what is dirty first), then `git branch -d claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a integration` (lowercase -d refuses anything unmerged — do NOT use -D). DO NOT delete `claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21`: it holds 2 commits not in master; review with `git log -p master..claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` and decide separately. Remote-branch pruning is a separate judgement call — origin branches may be someone's reference points.

challenge.reason: The branch arithmetic is all correct and independently verified (affectionate-raman 0, mystifying-kapitsa 2, integration 0, both origin branches 0; the dir-name/branch-name trap is real). But the headline claim — that the worktree is 17 MiB of stale duplicated checkout — is false. `git status --porcelain` in it returns 24 entries: 22 modified files (+428/-1) implementing BL-386 (Escape key actually dismissing 21 UI overlays, replacing a `_root` keydown listener that never fired when focus was on `<body>`), plus TWO UNTRACKED test files, `agent/tests/test_overlay_escape.py` (45 lines) and `agent/ui/tools/test_overlay_escape.mjs` (203 lines). "BL-386" greps to 0 hits in master's codex.js and is absent from master, claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21, and integration — this work is committed nowhere. Untracked files have never been staged, so no reflog/stash/dangling-object path recovers them; `--force` destroys them permanently. The recommendation's hedge actively steers into this: "add --force only if it reports dirty state" inverts the safety rule (dirty state is the reason to STOP, not to escalate), and quick_win:true / effort:minutes frames it as a no-thought cleanup. Correct sequence: (1) in the worktree, `git checkout -b rescue/bl-386-overlay-escape && git add -A && git commit` to make the 430 lines recoverable; (2) review the fix and decide merge-or-discard on its merits — it looks like a genuine a11y fix with tests, which this repo's "built but never plugged in" history says is worth landing; (3) only then `git worktree remove` and `git branch -d claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a integration` (lowercase -d, which correctly refuses unmerged). Never delete `claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21` (2 unmerged commits) — the recommendation is right to flag that. Remote pruning is a separate call and not urgent. Reclassified INVESTIGATE/hours: someone must actually read 24 changed files before 17 MiB is reclaimed, and 17 MiB is a trivial prize next to losing an untracked test suite.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Executed as written with --force: permanent loss of an uncommitted BL-386 fix across 22 files (+428 lines) making Escape dismiss 21 UI overlays, plus 248 lines of brand-new test coverage in two UNTRACKED files (agent/tests/test_overlay_escape.py, agent/ui/tools/test_overlay_escape.mjs) that exist in no commit, no branch, and no reflog — unrecoverable by any git means. Executed without --force: `git worktree remove` simply refuses on the dirty tree, so the "minutes" quick win does not happen and the operator is left guessing at the next step. Name-matched deletion (the trap the recommendation correctly warns about) would additionally destroy the 2 unmerged commits on claude/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21.

title: Remove the two dead CDN fallbacks in index.html — already vendored locally, and they phone home
rationale: agent/ui/index.html loads marked.min.js and highlight.min.js as vendored local files with plain synchronous <script> tags at lines 14-15, so window.marked and window.hljs are ALWAYS defined by the time the IIFE at line 25 runs its `needMarked`/`needHl` checks. The cdnjs fallbacks at lines 56-58 are therefore unreachable in the normal path — and in the abnormal path (a vendored file fails to load) they silently fetch from cdnjs.cloudflare.com, breaking the local-first guarantee without telling the operator. The github-dark.min.css fallback is doubly redundant: that exact stylesheet is already vendored at agent/ui/vendor/css/github-dark.min.css and hard-linked at line 8. This precise cleanup was already performed for DOMPurify — line 60 carries the comment 'DOMPurify CDN fallback removed — vendored locally'. This finishes a job someone already started and documented.
evidence: agent/ui/index.html:14-16 vendored <script src="/layla-ui/vendor/js/{marked,highlight,purify}.min.js"> loaded synchronously; fallback IIFE opens at :25; :56 `_loadScript('https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/.../marked.min.js')`; :57 `_loadCss('https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/.../github-dark.min.css')`; :58 `_loadScript('https://cdnjs.cloudflare.com/.../highlight.min.js')`; :60 comment recording the identical DOMPurify removal. Local copies confirmed tracked: marked.min.js 36060 B, highlight.min.js 122939 B, vendor/css/github-dark.min.css 1324 B. Line 8 already links the vendored CSS.
risk: Low, but verify before deleting rather than after. Removing the fallbacks means a corrupted or missing vendored asset degrades to no-syntax-highlighting instead of silently reaching the internet — which is the correct behaviour for a local-first product and arguably the reason to do it. Confirm the vendored files are actually served at /layla-ui/vendor/... in the static mount before removing the safety net. Roughly 8 lines deleted, plus the now-unused _loadScript/_loadCss helpers if nothing else calls them (check first — I did not verify other callers).

challenge.reason: VERIFIED, and the case is stronger than stated. (1) Vendored assets confirmed at agent/ui/vendor/js/{marked.min.js 36060B, highlight.min.js 122939B, purify.min.js 21108B} and agent/ui/vendor/css/github-dark.min.css 1324B; agent/main.py:1134 mounts the whole agent/ui tree at /layla-ui via StaticFiles(_UI_DIR = AGENT_DIR/"ui"), so /layla-ui/vendor/... resolves — the "confirm the static mount serves them" caveat is discharged. (2) The three synchronous <script> tags at index.html:14-16 run before the IIFE at :25, so needMarked/needHl are false in every normal load. (3) The unverified caveat about other callers is now closed: a repo-wide ripgrep for `_loadScript|_loadCss` returns exactly 5 hits, all in agent/ui/index.html — the two definitions (:27, :39) and the three CDN call sites (:55, :57, :58). Nothing else calls them, so both helpers are safely deletable with the fallbacks. (4) The decisive fact the recommendation missed: agent/main.py:688-700 sets a default CSP of `default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'` applied by the middleware at :709. Under the app as normally served, the cdnjs fetches are BLOCKED BY THE BROWSER — they cannot succeed even in the degraded path. So the fallbacks are not a working safety net that we are trading away for purity; they are zero-value dead code in the default configuration. They can only actually fire in two edge cases: the operator sets `security_headers_enabled: false` in runtime_config, or the page is served by the dev static preview server (agent/ui/_preview_server.py, which sends no CSP header — only Cache-Control at :52). Both are exactly the cases where a silent internet fetch is most surprising. Nothing in agent/tests, docs, or scripts references cdnjs or asserts the fallbacks exist. Effort of "minutes" is honest for one maintainer: ~8 lines of fallback plus ~26 lines of now-orphaned helper, in one file, no build step. ONE ADDENDUM the recommendation omits: agent/ui/sw.js precaches "/ui/" (index.html) in PRECACHE at :38-51, and its header comment states a CACHE bump is required for index.html changes to reach existing installs. The edit must bump `const CACHE = "layla-ui-v34"` (sw.js:37) to v35 or existing browsers keep serving the old index.html. agent/tests/test_product_claims_honesty.py:241 enforces this class of staleness guard.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Nothing in the normal path — the fallback branch is unreachable when the vendored files load, and CSP-blocked when they don't. The only behavioural delta is in the already-broken scenario where a vendored asset is missing or corrupt: today, with CSP on (the default), the page loses markdown rendering and syntax highlighting and logs a CSP violation; after the change it loses the same things and logs a plain load error. Identical user-visible outcome, clearer diagnostics. With CSP disabled or under the preview server, the change converts a silent successful internet fetch into a visible local failure — which is the correct behaviour for a local-first product. Risk of forgetting the sw.js CACHE bump: existing PWA installs continue serving the cached v34 index.html, so the change appears not to have taken effect. That is the one way to get this wrong, and it is cheap to avoid.

title: Reclaim ~49 MiB of untracked working-tree clutter (zero git risk)
rationale: None of this is tracked and all of it is already correctly gitignored, so this is pure disk hygiene with no repository consequence — which is precisely why it is a safe quick win. Measured: backups/ 25M (8 entries: two .db snapshots from Jul 5 and Jul 18 plus -shm/-wal siblings and two vectors_* dirs), .claude/worktrees 17M (see the worktree item — use git worktree remove, not rm), build/ 5.3M and dist/ 1.2M (PyInstaller output), agent/.governance 2.4M (runtime governance state incl. the live audit.log), agent/.layla 181K, .ruff_cache 152K + agent/.pytest_cache 352K, layla.egg-info 62K, and 58 __pycache__ dirs outside the venvs. Two stray root databases also sit uncommitted: german_mode.db (24 KiB, Jul 3) and language_tutor.db (24 KiB, Jul 15) — both caught by `*.db`, but their presence at the repo root suggests a tool defaulting its DB path to CWD rather than a configured data dir, which is worth a look.
evidence: du -sh: backups/ 25M, .claude/worktrees 17M, build/ 5.3M, dist/ 1.2M, agent/.governance 2.4M, agent/.layla 181K, agent/.pytest_cache 352K, .ruff_cache 152K, layla.egg-info 62K. `find . -name __pycache__ -type d -not -path './.venv*' | wc -l` -> 58. Root DBs: german_mode.db 24576 B (Jul 3 23:51), language_tutor.db 24576 B (Jul 15 23:15), layla.db 4075520 B. `git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all` -> exactly one row (?? .planning/PROJECT.md), confirming none of the above is tracked or staged.
risk: LOW on git, but read before running anything: backups/ 25M contains the ONLY snapshots of layla.db from Jul 5 and Jul 18 — the operator's actual conversation and memory data. Do not blanket-delete; at most drop the older Jul 5 snapshot after confirming the Jul 18 one is good, and leave that to the operator. build/ and dist/ regenerate from PyInstaller; caches and __pycache__ regenerate free. Do NOT delete agent/.governance (2.4M) — it holds the LIVE audit log the running app appends to. Safe subset, ~7 MiB: build/, dist/, layla.egg-info/, .ruff_cache/, .pytest_cache/, agent/.pytest_cache/, and the 58 __pycache__ dirs.

challenge.reason: HOLDS, but only in its own narrowed form — the title must be corrected from ~49 MiB to ~7 MiB and .claude/worktrees removed from scope entirely. Verified independently: all nine size measurements are accurate, the 58 __pycache__ count is accurate, and layla.egg-info is genuinely inert because the venv uses a PEP-660 editable install (__editable__.layla-1.4.0.pth + __editable___layla_1_4_0_finder.py in .venv/Lib/site-packages/), which never reads .egg-info — so deleting it cannot break the `layla` CLI entry point. The safe subset (build/, dist/, layla.egg-info/, .ruff_cache/, .pytest_cache/, agent/.pytest_cache/, 58 __pycache__ dirs) is regenerable, gitignored, and safe to delete in minutes by one maintainer. THREE CORRECTIONS. (1) .claude/worktrees (17M) is NOT clutter and NOT zero-git-risk: `git worktree list` shows C:/Work/Programming/Layla/.claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21 as a LIVE registered worktree on branch claude/affectionate-raman-8ff02a containing 22 modified files (+428 insertions, -1 deletion, all agent/ui/components/*.js) plus 2 untracked files, and `git log master..HEAD` is EMPTY — those 428 lines exist on disk and nowhere in git history. `git worktree remove` refuses a dirty worktree without --force, so git does protect the operator, but counting 17M toward a headline reclaim is exactly what pushes someone to reach for --force or rm -rf when the command refuses. Exclude outright, not footnote. (2) The 49 MiB headline collapses to the rec's own stated ~7 MiB safe subset: 17M is live uncommitted work, 25M is the operator's only DB snapshots, 2.4M is the live governance/audit log — the rec excludes all three in its risk field while still counting them in the title. (3) Evidence error: the rec claims `git status --porcelain --untracked-files=all` returns exactly one row (?? .planning/PROJECT.md); it now returns ZERO rows. Stale, and that stale probe is what hid finding (1) — a clean main-worktree status says nothing about a linked worktree's state. Separately, the rec is right to stop at "worth a look" on the root DBs: AST-adjacent check shows agent/services/infrastructure/german_mode.py:83-89 and language_tutor.py:54-57 resolve those paths at runtime (falling back to layla_data_file(...)), and agent/ui/main.js:612 documents that the two panels write disjoint DBs. They are live German-mode and language-tutor learning decks whose path resolution landed in CWD — a path-resolution bug to fix, not clutter to sweep, and they must be shielded from any `*.db` glob.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Executed as scoped in the corrected form (~7 MiB), nothing breaks: build/ and dist/ regenerate from the packaging build, .ruff_cache/.pytest_cache/__pycache__ regenerate free on next run, and layla.egg-info is unused by the PEP-660 editable install. Executed as titled (~49 MiB), three things break. Worst: forcing removal of .claude/worktrees/mystifying-kapitsa-58fd21 destroys 428 uncommitted lines across 22 agent/ui/components/*.js files plus 2 untracked files that exist in no commit anywhere — unrecoverable. Second: blanket-deleting backups/ destroys the only snapshots of the operator's conversation and memory data (layla_20260718_213249.db 3.5 MB, layla_20260705_200424.db 675 KB) against a live layla.db of 4.07 MB. Third: deleting agent/.governance discards the live audit log the running app appends to. A careless `*.db` sweep at the repo root would additionally wipe german_mode.db and language_tutor.db, losing the user's vocabulary and SRS decks that german_mode.py and language_tutor.py resolve and read at runtime.

title: Decide the status of CASTILLA_RELEASE_PLAN.md and REMEDIATION_PLAN.md — both orphaned from the canonical plan
rationale: The operator asked which .planning docs are live vs historical. Measured answer: PLAN.md is LIVE (18 commits, self-declared 'single source of truth'), BACKLOG.md is LIVE (113 commits, most-edited planning doc, 125 items still marked open which its own header explains as externally-blocked or parked — do NOT archive it despite its 190 KiB). The other two are orphans: CASTILLA_RELEASE_PLAN.md (34 KiB, 1 commit, still labelled 'Status: DRAFT for review', dated 2026-07-07 and framed as preceding the v1.0.0 tag) and REMEDIATION_PLAN.md (6 KiB, 1 commit, 2026-07-09) have ZERO inbound references from anywhere in the repo — including from PLAN.md, the very doc claiming to supersede scattered plan docs. I mark this INVESTIGATE rather than ARCHIVE for one specific reason: REMEDIATION_PLAN.md records the operator's explicit voice-calibration decisions (EDGE~6, NERVE~7, SIGNAL~4, IRON~4, 'direct but keep some warmth') and the non-negotiables list. That is live design intent, not history, and archiving it unlinked would bury a decision the codebase still depends on. Separately: .planning/PROJECT.md is currently UNTRACKED and was created today, after being deliberately deleted in commit 59f24b4 ('fold all planning docs into one canonical PLAN.md; clean .planning/') — the consolidation is quietly being undone.
evidence: `rg -l --fixed-strings CASTILLA_RELEASE_PLAN.md .` -> 0 rows; same for REMEDIATION_PLAN.md -> 0 rows. Commit counts: PLAN.md 18 (last 2026-07-05), BACKLOG.md 113 (last 2026-07-17), CASTILLA_RELEASE_PLAN.md 1 (2026-07-07), REMEDIATION_PLAN.md 1 (2026-07-09). BACKLOG status parse (per-item markers, assertion-guarded, asserted >100 matches): 128 of 275 distinct BL ids carry a status marker -> 125 open, 2 partial, 1 done. CASTILLA header literal: 'Status: DRAFT for review'. PROJECT.md: `git ls-files .planning/PROJECT.md` -> empty (untracked), while `git log` shows 4 commits ending in 59f24b4 with status D. Related shipped work: .planning/phases/13-castilla-repair/{CONTEXT,VERIFICATION}.md.
risk: The risk is acting too fast, not too slow. If you archive REMEDIATION_PLAN.md without first lifting its voice-calibration decisions into PLAN.md, you lose the rationale behind the persona tuning and someone re-litigates it in three months. Recommended sequence: (1) add a Superseded-plans section to PLAN.md linking both docs so they stop being orphans; (2) lift the REMEDIATION_PLAN operator-decisions block into PLAN.md proper; (3) only then `git mv` the Castilla plan to docs/archive/ if its phase-13 work is confirmed shipped. Also decide deliberately whether .planning/PROJECT.md should exist at all — it was removed on purpose once and is currently one `git add -A` from being re-committed against that decision.

title: Do NOT delete these four — verified legitimate, flagged to survive the next cleanup sweep
rationale: Each pattern-matches as bloat and each is legitimate on inspection. Recording the evidence so a future automated sweep does not remove them. (1) benchmarks/scorecard_qwen2.5-coder-{3b,7b}{,-hard}.json — 4 files, ~1.5 KiB each, deliberate tracked pass@1 baselines documented in benchmarks/README.md as REQ-74 with a results table and an honest-reading section admitting both tiers are saturated. The live scorecard that DOES churn (scorecard_live.json) is already gitignored, so the split is intentional and correct. (2) agent/tools/nssm.exe — 368 KiB committed Windows binary, MIT licensed, invoked by four real installer scripts; vendoring it is what lets Windows service install work offline, which is the local-first promise. (3) desktop/dist/index.html — despite the dist/ path, it is a 1031-byte hand-written Tauri fallback page with an explanatory comment, not generated output; the root /dist/ gitignore rule is anchored with a leading slash specifically so it does not swallow this. (4) readme-assets/ — 1.6 MiB (demo.gif 754 KiB + three PNGs) is the largest tracked asset group but is what makes the README legible for a public repo.
evidence: benchmarks/README.md documents the scorecards as 'Tracked pass@1 scorecards from scripts/benchmark_coding.py (REQ-74)' with a baseline table; scorecard_live.json is matched by the .gitignore rule 'benchmarks/scorecard_live.json'. nssm.exe callers: uninstall.ps1:68-70, agent/install-autostart.ps1:22-26, agent/install/uninstall_service.ps1:9-44, agent/install/install_service.ps1:1-10 (the last comments 'NSSM: bundled in tools/nssm.exe (MIT license, ~400KB)'). desktop/dist/index.html contains the comment 'Fallback shown only if the local server isn't reachable'; .gitignore uses '/dist/' (anchored), not 'dist/'. readme-assets sizes: demo.gif 754115 B, screenshot-web-ui.png 298015 B, approvals-panel.png 297889 B, hero-layla-ui.png 289189 B.
risk: The risk here is inaction-shaped: none of this needs doing. The reason to record it is that three of the four (a dist/ path, a committed .exe, a benchmarks output dir) are exactly what a pattern-matching cleanup deletes, and nssm.exe in particular would silently break Windows service install for every downstream user with no test catching it. If anything is actioned, the only defensible one is compressing readme-assets/demo.gif, trading repo size against demo quality on a public README.

title: Delete 4 dependencies that are imported nowhere in live code
rationale: requests, orjson and diskcache are already annotated as 'FINDING (dead dep)' inside the repo's own parity gate but were never removed — the gate records the debt instead of clearing it. hypothesis is a fourth instance the gate structurally cannot catch, because it lives in [dev] and the gate's direction-B scope is the [all] closure only (test_dependency_parity.py docstring, 'DELIBERATE SCOPE LIMITS'). requests is superseded by httpx, which IS used. Removing all four also shrinks the [core]/[cpu]/[dev] install for every user. Keep python-multipart (FastAPI imports it internally as `multipart` for form parsing) and bandit (invoked as a subprocess via `[sys.executable, '-m', 'bandit', ...]` at layla/tools/impl/code.py:379) — both are correctly annotated already.
evidence: AST scan of 946 files under agent/ (0 parse failures, utf-8-sig): top-level import names for requests/orjson/diskcache/hypothesis appear 0 times in src and 0 times in tests. Independently confirmed by ripgrep '^\s*(import|from) <mod>\b' over agent/ = 0 hits each, and repo-wide outside agent/ = 0 hits each. Only textual matches are false positives (schemas.requests module import at routers/agent.py:22, 'requests per minute' at main.py:1324). Pre-annotated at agent/tests/test_dependency_parity.py:82-88. hypothesis IS installed in .venv-test but used by no test file.
risk: Low. The parity gate's _NOT_IMPORTED_OK allowlist has a burn-down test (test_resolution_allowlist_burns_down) that will fail if an entry is left for a dist no longer declared — so the three annotations must be deleted in the same commit as the deps. hypothesis needs no annotation change ([dev] is out of that gate's scope). Small chance a transitive consumer relied on requests being present implicitly; pip will still pull it for any dep that truly needs it.

challenge.reason: Independently verified with my own AST probe over 2436 .py files repo-wide (not just agent/; build/ included, 0 parse failures): requests, orjson, diskcache and hypothesis have ZERO import sites. A literal-string scan for "import <mod>" across ALL file types (docs, JSON, PowerShell, templates, notebooks) also returns zero, ruling out exec'd snippets and dynamic-import name lists; the __import__/importlib call sites in install/checks.py, dependency_recovery.py, system_doctor.py and setup_wizard.py were each inspected and name none of the four. No user-facing surface: the "requests>=2.28" in agent/docs/SKILL_PACKS.md is a pack-manifest EXAMPLE, and services/skills/skill_sandbox.py pip-installs pack deps into the pack's OWN venv, so skill packs are unaffected. No installer script (install/*.ps1, *.sh, INSTALL.bat) references any of them. hypothesis appears in no test, pytest.ini, conftest, CI workflow, or planning doc. TWO CORRECTIONS. (1) The install-size rationale is FALSE: a reverse-dependency scan of .venv shows all three survive transitively regardless — diskcache is an UNCONDITIONAL requirement of llama-cpp-python, requests is unconditional for huggingface_hub/instructor/tiktoken (so it persists even in [dev], which has no sentence-transformers), and orjson arrives via langchain-text-splitters -> langchain-core -> langsmith. Nobody's install shrinks. The real benefit is declaration parity and license-accounting honesty (THIRD_PARTY.md:51 credits diskcache as a direct dep). (2) The change set as written goes RED: test_ci_installed_dists_are_reachable_from_an_extra (direction A, test_dependency_parity.py:260) fails if agent/requirements.txt still lists a dist no extra declares, so those lines must be deleted in the SAME commit as the pyproject entries and the three _NOT_IMPORTED_OK annotations. Effort stays "minutes" for one maintainer.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Nothing at runtime. All three dists remain present in any real install via transitive requirements (llama-cpp-python -> diskcache; huggingface_hub/instructor/tiktoken -> requests; langsmith -> orjson), so even a code path I failed to detect would still find the module importable. hypothesis is used by zero tests and is absent from requirements.txt and every CI workflow, so no test run changes. The concrete breakage risk is CI going red on an INCOMPLETE edit: leaving the entries in agent/requirements.txt fails direction A (test_ci_installed_dists_are_reachable_from_an_extra); leaving the three _NOT_IMPORTED_OK annotations fails test_resolution_allowlist_burns_down. Stale prose left behind unless fixed in the same pass: agent/services/infrastructure/retry_util.py:3 (docstring claims diskcache is "already adopted" — it is not), the requests comment in agent/requirements.txt (claims download_docs.py uses it; that file imports only stdlib), agent/docs/INSTALL_PROFILES.md lines 33/50/100/116/137, and THIRD_PARTY.md:51. Nothing in the operator's protected remote pillar (clustering, multi-device, phone, skill-packs, Discord) touches any of the four.

title: Wire the existing check_api_contracts.py into CI as a ratchet — it already works and nothing calls it
rationale: The repo already contains a correct, purpose-built AST gate that verifies every endpoint has a test, that every router is registered in main.py, and that every route has a docstring. It runs, it reports accurately, and it returns the right exit code — but it appears in no workflow file, so it has never gated anything. This is the codebase's signature defect applied to the very tool built to detect that defect, and it is the cheapest structural fix available. Wire it at the CURRENT number (181) rather than its stale 125, so the surface can only improve; then walk the threshold down. Do not try to close 181 gaps first — that turns a minutes-long fix into a weeks-long one and the gate stays unwired in the meantime.
evidence: agent/scripts/check_api_contracts.py exists and its docstring states the exact contract. Executed read-only from agent/: prints 'NOTE: 181 uncovered routes exceeds threshold 125; 56 above limit', 'MISSING DOCSTRINGS (124 routes)', 'FAIL: 56 issue(s) found'; measured true exit code = 1 (verified without a pipe, since piping to tail masked it). ripgrep for 'check_api_contracts' across .github/workflows/*.yml returns zero hits, while check_copyleft.py by contrast appears at ci.yml:48 and ci.yml:112.
risk: Low if the threshold is set to today's 181 — the gate then blocks only NEW uncovered endpoints. Setting it to 125 makes CI red immediately, and a permanently-red gate gets deselected, which the parity test's own docstring warns is worse than an honest limit.
area: Dependency + API surface hygiene (pyproject extras, requirements.txt, CI pin discipline, licence posture, routers/ HTTP surface)

title: Make the no-torch embedder path reachable: [cpu]-only model2vec/sqlite-vec are in no documented install
rationale: vector_store.py deliberately prefers model2vec static embeddings — 'no torch, ~30k/s CPU, ~92% MiniLM quality' — as the potato-tier default, falling back to sentence-transformers only when model2vec is absent. But model2vec and sqlite-vec are declared ONLY in [cpu], and [all] expands core,llm,voice,vision,crawl,research,data,docs,viz,nlp,security,tui,network,tray,watcher — it never references [cpu]. So no user running any documented install command can obtain the fast path, and the fallback branch is the only branch that ships. On this specific 4-core/16GB box that is the single largest measurable win in my area: it removes a 493 MB dependency from the install and takes the embedder off torch entirely. The parity gate explicitly scoped [cpu] out of enforcement ('fixing them means resolving a profile conflict'), so this will not self-surface. Resolve it by adding model2vec+sqlite-vec to [core] (they are small and pure-Python-ish) or by adding a [cpu]-inclusive install target to the docs and installer.
evidence: pyproject.toml:78-79 declares model2vec and sqlite-vec under [cpu] only. Computed [all] closure = 56 dists; membership check: model2vec in[all]=False extras=['cpu']; sqlite-vec in[all]=False extras=['cpu']. Live .venv probe via importlib.util.find_spec: model2vec NOT INSTALLED, sqlite_vec NO, chromadb NO, while torch = 493.0 MB and sentence_transformers present. agent/layla/memory/vector_store.py:204 '# Potato-tier default: model2vec static embeddings (no torch)'; :214 'from model2vec import StaticModel'; :224 'model2vec unavailable (%s); falling back to sentence-transformers'.
risk: Medium. model2vec produces different embedding dimensions/vectors than MiniLM, so an existing vector store built with sentence-transformers will not be comparable — switching the default likely needs a re-embed or a dimension-keyed store path. Verify _embedder_dim handling and whether the fallback store keys on model name before flipping the default. Adding the deps to [core] without changing preference order is the safe first step.

title: Triage the 5 fully-orphaned routers (15 endpoints) — wire plugins/vision, decide on automation/feedback/learning_verification
rationale: Five router modules have zero callers of any kind: no UI fetch, no test, no in-repo client. Per the operator's standing constraint these are NOT a blanket delete — plugins.py belongs to the skill-packs/plugin pillar that is explicitly WANTED and must be wired, and vision.py plausibly backs a real capability. But automation.py (a rules engine with add/enable/delete/emit), feedback.py and learning_verification.py are the classic 'complete component, no caller' shape this codebase keeps producing, and they are small enough that finishing them is cheap. The point of this item is that the decision must be made deliberately per-router rather than left to drift — right now each one is inert code that the capability manifest may nonetheless advertise.
evidence: AST route extraction over agent/routers/*.py + main.py, cross-referenced against a UI corpus (68 .js + 1 .html, 1,115,791 chars, sanity-asserted to contain '/chat'), a test corpus (2,329,604 chars), and a 654-file in-repo client corpus with agent/routers/ EXCLUDED (an earlier run wrongly reported other=True because prefix-less routers self-match their own decorator literal). Fully orphaned: automation.py 5/5, feedback.py 3/3, plugins.py 3/3, vision.py 2/2, learning_verification.py 2/2. Sizes: 77, 36, 42, 30, 19 lines = 204 total. Independently re-verified by ripgrep for 'automation', 'vision/analyze', 'mood/signal', 'world/summary', 'manual/summary', 'feedback/stats', 'memory/conflicts', 'timeline/episodes' across the whole repo excluding agent/routers/ — zero endpoint references; only the word 'automation' in prose in README.md and LAYLA_NORTH_STAR.md.
risk: Deleting plugins.py would damage a pillar the operator has said must be completed, not cut. Deleting automation.py may remove a half-built feature that other planning docs assume exists. Conversely, leaving them mounted means the app advertises endpoints that no client exercises and no test protects — if the capability manifest enumerates routes, these become advertised lies.

challenge.reason:
The endpoint-orphan measurement is correct and I reproduced it — 15 endpoints across the 5 routers have no UI, test, or in-repo client caller. But the rationale mischaracterizes two of the five at the capability level, and that error sets the effort and kind wrongly.

(1) automation.py is NOT "a complete component with no caller." agent/services/memory/knowledge_watcher.py:277 calls dispatch_event("file_modified", {...}) on every ingest — the rules engine has a live in-process producer and runs today. Only rule AUTHORING lacks a surface, and rules are already authorable via the local-only endpoints.
(2) vision.py backs a capability that is already wired: analyze_image is a registered agent tool (agent/layla/tools/domains/analysis.py, agent/layla/tools/impl/analysis.py, covered by test_vision.py and test_tool_feature_gating.py). The router is a duplicate HTTP facade, not an undecided feature.
(3) plugins.py is operator-ruled WANTED (skill-packs pillar) — already not up for decision.

So three of the five need no investigation at all, which makes "INVESTIGATE / days" wrong in both kind and effort. Only learning_verification.py (19 lines) is inert at both layers.

The item also buries the one genuinely valuable finding. agent/services/prompts/system_head_builder.py:1331 calls feedback_hint_for_prompt() and injects it into memory_sections["answer_feedback"] on every turn — the READ path is fully wired into the prompt. record_feedback, the WRITE path, has no producer anywhere in the repo except the orphaned router and tests. The prompt section is therefore structurally guaranteed to be empty forever. That is this codebase's signature defect exactly, and it is a concrete hours-scale wiring job (a thumbs up/down control that POSTs /feedback, no build step), not a days-long triage.

Probe defect, per the standing evidence rule: the claimed independent re-verification searched for 'mood/signal', 'world/summary', 'manual/summary', 'memory/conflicts', and 'timeline/episodes' — none of which are routes on any of these five routers — and never searched the actual learning_verification prefix, which is /memory/verification. That confirmation step verified nothing about the router it claimed to cover.

Corrected item: STRENGTHEN — wire the answer-feedback write path (UI control -> POST /feedback) to close a loop already plumbed into the prompt. Effort: hours. Handle the rest as trivia alongside it: delete or keep vision.py's facade (capability is live either way), leave automation.py alone (it works), keep plugins.py (operator ruling), and make a one-minute call on learning_verification.py's 19 lines.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Acting on the item as written risks the maintainer "deciding the fate" of automation.py, whose dispatch_event is called live from knowledge_watcher.py on every file ingest — removing or gating it would silently kill working machinery, exactly the failure this audit is meant to prevent. Treating vision.py as an undecided feature risks touching a router whose underlying capability is already exposed through the tool registry, where the real (harmless) redundancy is only the 30-line HTTP facade. The corrected STRENGTHEN item breaks nothing: adding a feedback control is additive, the endpoint and service already exist and are tested (test_answer_feedback.py), and the prompt-side consumer already handles the empty case via a try/except that falls through to no section.

title: Pin ruff in CI — it is installed completely unbounded and has already caused a silent lint failure
rationale: The lint job runs `pip install ruff` with no version constraint whatsoever, so every CI run resolves whatever ruff published most recently. Ruff changes default rule selection and rule behaviour between minor releases; an upgrade can either fail the build on code that did not change, or (worse, and per the brief this already happened) silently stop enforcing something. A single-line pin makes lint results reproducible and turns ruff upgrades into a deliberate, reviewable change. This is the highest value-to-effort ratio item in the whole dependency area.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:207-208 — 'Install ruff' / 'run: pip install ruff', no specifier. ci.yml:211 then runs 'python -m ruff check agent fabrication_assist'. The local .venv resolves ruff 0.15.20. requirements.txt line 'ruff>=0.4' is commented out, so it constrains nothing. Config lives at pyproject.toml [tool.ruff] with target-version py311 and 4 ignored rules.
risk: Very low. The only risk is pinning to a version whose rule set differs from what the code currently satisfies — pin to the version that is green today (0.15.x) and confirm one clean run before merging.

title: Migrate duckduckgo-search to ddgs — the package was renamed upstream and web_search is inert today
rationale: Upstream renamed both the GitHub project and the PyPI distribution from duckduckgo-search to ddgs; the old distribution still resolves but is the frozen legacy line and emits a RuntimeWarning telling users to migrate. The import name changes too (duckduckgo_search -> ddgs), so this is a dependency rename AND a one-line code change, not just a pin bump. Independently, neither package is installed in the live .venv, so the web_search tool currently returns its 'not installed' error path on every call — worth knowing before attributing any search failure to model behaviour. Implement as a try-ddgs/except-fall-back-to-duckduckgo_search import so both resolutions work during the transition.
evidence: pyproject.toml:133 'duckduckgo-search>=6.0' under [research]; agent/requirements.txt same. agent/layla/tools/impl/web.py:203 'from duckduckgo_search import DDGS', :204 'with DDGS() as ddgs', :216 error string 'duckduckgo-search not installed: pip install duckduckgo-search'. Live .venv importlib probe: duckduckgo_search NOT INSTALLED and ddgs NOT INSTALLED. Rename confirmed against PyPI and upstream issue reports (pydantic-ai #2182, langchain #31892).
oss_candidate: ddgs (PyPI) — the maintained continuation of duckduckgo-search by the same author (deedy5), MIT-licensed, actively released, pure Python with no browser dependency. It is the same library under its current name, so it is strictly better than pinning the deprecated alias, and it is licence-clean for a non-commercial source-available product. No alternative search library is warranted here; the existing choice was right, only its name moved.
risk: Low-moderate. DuckDuckGo's HTML endpoints change and rate-limit, so the tool can fail at runtime for reasons unrelated to the package name — do not treat a green install as a working web_search. Verify with one live call before declaring it fixed, and keep the graceful 'not installed' path.

challenge.reason:
The action is right but two supporting claims are wrong. CONFIRMED: PyPI duckduckgo-search 8.1.1 carries an explicit 'renamed to ddgs' notice; ddgs 9.14.4 (2026-05-15) is MIT, actively released, py>=3.10, and API-compatible with the existing call site — DDGS still defines __enter__/__exit__ (verified in ddgs/ddgs.py) and text(self, query, **kwargs) accepts the current positional query plus region/max_results kwargs, so web.py:203-205 needs no restructuring. Deps are Windows-safe (primp is Rust but ships prebuilt win_amd64 wheels, MIT; lxml, httpx[brotli,http2,socks], fake-useragent, click) and the DHT/peer-network and API-server features are opt-in extras, so a base install stays local-first. Not a hand-rolled-vs-dependency trade and nothing wanted gets deleted, so no auto-hold applies.

DEFECT 1 — the claimed symptom does not occur. The rationale states web_search 'returns its not-installed error path on every call'. It does not. agent/services/agent/llm_decision.py:159 _drop_missing_dependency_tools withholds any tool whose registry `requires` module is unimportable from the set shown to the model; ddg_search declares requires='duckduckgo_search' (agent/layla/tools/domains/web.py:23), so on this box the tool is never offered and never called. agent/tests/test_capability_sources_honesty.py:261 asserts exactly that, and the docstring at llm_decision.py:167 cites 'duckduckgo-search not installed' as the failure mode it was built to prevent. The error string is unreachable here. The real state is simply that the whole optional `research` extra is uninstalled (trafilatura and bs4 are missing too, per importlib probe against C:\Work\Programming\Layla\.venv\Scripts\python.exe).

DEFECT 2 — the prescribed try-ddgs/except-duckduckgo_search dual import misfires against that gate. `requires` holds a single module string with no any-of semantics. Declaring duckduckgo_search means a box with only ddgs installed has a working tool withheld; declaring ddgs means the same for a legacy box. Either drop the fallback and go single-name, or teach the gate to accept a tuple.

SCOPE — not 'a one-line code change'. Seven-plus touch points: agent/layla/tools/impl/web.py:203,216; agent/layla/tools/domains/web.py:23; pyproject.toml:133; agent/requirements.txt:88; agent/services/infrastructure/dependency_recovery.py:33 (_PIP_ALLOWLIST — try_pip_install refuses unlisted names, so a stale entry silently breaks auto-recovery); the hardcoded expectation at test_capability_sources_honesty.py:244; plus agent/docs/INSTALL_PROFILES.md:56,103 and THIRD_PARTY.md:76. 'hours' remains honest for one maintainer.

KIND corrected from REPLACE_WITH_OSS to STRENGTHEN: nothing is being replaced — this hardens an existing correct integration against upstream deprecation. Also note region='wt-wt' is passed explicitly while ddgs 9.x defaults to 'us-en'; verify the old region code is still accepted with one live call, per the recommendation's own caveat that a green install does not prove a working search.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Low blast radius if the gate metadata is migrated in lockstep with the import. Specific breakages if done as written: (1) leaving requires='duckduckgo_search' in domains/web.py while the impl imports ddgs makes the tool invisible to the model on any box that installed only ddgs — a silent false-negative, the exact 'built well and never plugged in' failure mode, and it would not surface as an error anywhere; (2) test_capability_sources_honesty.py:244 hardcodes ddg_search -> 'duckduckgo_search' and fails the moment the manifest changes; (3) forgetting dependency_recovery.py:33 leaves the auto-install allowlist pointing at the deprecated distribution, so one-click recovery silently refuses to install the new name. Runtime risk to ddg_search itself is minimal — the context manager and text() signature both still hold in 9.x — but DuckDuckGo endpoint churn and rate limiting can still fail the tool for reasons unrelated to the rename, so keep the graceful not-installed path and confirm with one live call before declaring it fixed. No other subsystem imports duckduckgo_search; the playwright-based browser_search path (services/infrastructure/browser.py:206) scrapes html.duckduckgo.com directly and is entirely unaffected.

title: Add prometheus_client to [dev] and cover the prometheus-PRESENT branch of /metrics
rationale: I could not reproduce the claim that a missing prometheus_client breaks 8 CI tests, and I am reporting that rather than confirming it: every CI job installs agent/requirements.txt, which declares prometheus_client, and only one test file references it at all. The genuine defect is the mirror image. prometheus_client is in [core] and [cpu], so every real user has it and gets text/plain Prometheus output — but the only tests that exist deliberately exercise the ABSENT branch, and the package is installed in neither local venv. So the branch that ships is the branch nothing tests, which is precisely the failure mode the parity gate's direction-B was written to catch. Add prometheus_client to [dev] so `pip install layla[dev]` reproduces the shipping configuration, and add one test asserting /metrics returns text/plain rather than JSON when it is present.
evidence: pyproject.toml:62 and :96 declare prometheus_client under [core] and [cpu]; the [dev] block (lines 177-203) omits it. Computed closure: prometheus-client in[all]=True, extras=['core','cpu'] — absent from dev. importlib probe: prometheus_client NO in BOTH .venv and .venv-test. Only referencing test file is agent/tests/test_observability.py (14 test functions), whose docstring at :4 reads 'Metrics service (fallback mode — no prometheus_client required)' and :29 'Tests for the lightweight fallback metrics when prometheus_client is absent.' The branch point is agent/routers/metrics.py get_metrics(): 'if PROMETHEUS_AVAILABLE: return PlainTextResponse(..., media_type="text/plain; version=0.0.4")' else JSONResponse.
risk: Low. Installing prometheus_client into the dev/test environment flips PROMETHEUS_AVAILABLE to True, which may change the behaviour the existing 14 fallback tests assert — those tests will need to force the fallback explicitly (monkeypatch PROMETHEUS_AVAILABLE) rather than relying on the package being absent. That is a small edit but it must be done in the same change or the suite goes red.

title: Add upper bounds to the highest-churn unpinned dependencies
rationale: 52 of 65 declared dists carry a lower bound only. For a one-maintainer project this means any upstream major release can break a fresh install with no code change on this side — and the duckduckgo-search rename is the concrete proof that it already happened once. The project clearly knows the pattern: fastapi, uvicorn, chromadb, sentence-transformers, llama-cpp-python, numpy, apscheduler, langchain-text-splitters and outlines all carry proper `<` bounds. The discipline just was not applied uniformly. Do not bound all 52 — that creates a resolver maintenance burden with no payoff. Bound the ones with a history of breaking majors and a real blast radius: litellm (very high release cadence, gateway-critical), playwright, trafilatura, matplotlib, scikit-learn, scipy, yfinance, textual, and the pytest plugin trio. Everything else can stay open.
evidence: Programmatic scan of pyproject.toml optional-dependencies: 65 distinct dists, 52 with no '<' in the specifier. Notable unbounded: litellm>=1.40.0 [llm], playwright>=1.40 [crawl], trafilatura>=1.6 [crawl], matplotlib>=3.8 [viz], scikit-learn>=1.4 [data], scipy>=1.12 [data], yfinance>=0.2 [data], textual>=0.40 [tui], keyring>=24 [core,cpu], torchao>=0.6 [core,cpu], pytest>=8.0 / pytest-asyncio>=0.23 / pytest-cov>=5.0 / pytest-timeout>=2.3 [dev]. Contrast with correctly bounded entries at pyproject.toml:33 fastapi>=0.115,<1 and :44-46 sentence-transformers>=3.0,<4, chromadb>=0.6.0,<1.
risk: Over-pinning is a real cost: too-tight upper bounds cause resolver conflicts on a machine that also has other packages, and create recurring bump chores for a solo maintainer. agent/requirements-lock.txt already exists and may be the better mechanism for reproducibility — check whether it is generated and used before duplicating that job in pyproject.
area: Dependency + API surface hygiene (pyproject extras, requirements.txt, CI pin discipline, licence posture, routers/ HTTP surface)

title: Keep torchao — it is 5.8 MB, not the source of the 493 MB torch install
rationale: Answering the operator's direct question, and correcting my own initial hypothesis. I expected torchao in [core]/[cpu] to be the thing dragging a multi-hundred-megabyte tensor stack into a CPU-only install. Measurement says otherwise: torchao itself is 5.8 MB, and torch's 493 MB arrives via sentence-transformers, which [core] and [cpu] both declare independently. Removing torchao would therefore save ~6 MB while giving up genuine int8 dynamic quantization of the embedder on CPU — a real speedup on this hardware. It is also fully guarded by try/except and degrades cleanly when absent. The lever on the 493 MB is model2vec (see the separate recommendation), not torchao. Recording this explicitly so the question does not get re-litigated and so no one 'optimizes' by deleting the wrong package.
evidence: Measured on the live .venv by summing file sizes under each package directory: torch = 493.0 MB (.venv/Lib/site-packages/torch), torchao = 5.8 MB, sentence_transformers = 1.9 MB. Installed versions: torch 2.12.1+cpu, torchao 0.17.0. Single usage site: agent/layla/memory/vector_store.py:267 'from torchao.quantization import Int8DynamicActivationInt8WeightConfig, quantize_' inside a try, with :270-272 'except Exception ... Embedder: no quantization (torchao unavailable: %s)'. AST scan confirms torchao src imports = 1, test imports = 0.
risk: None from leaving it alone. The risk is in acting on the intuition instead of the measurement — deleting torchao removes a CPU speedup and saves nothing meaningful.

title: Close test coverage on the 18 routers with zero tested endpoints that ARE UI-wired
rationale: This is a distinct population from the orphans and must not be conflated with them. Eighteen routers have no tested endpoint at all, but several are fully wired into the UI — onboarding (7/7 endpoints called from the UI), macros (5/5), obsidian (6/6), learn (7/8), improvements (4/4), goals (4/5), learned_skills (4/5), decisions (3/4). These are live, user-reachable surfaces with no regression protection whatsoever, which is a materially worse risk than dead code: dead code cannot break a user's session. Prioritise onboarding and learn — onboarding is the first-run path where a regression is most damaging and least likely to be noticed by a maintainer who never re-onboards.
evidence: Route/caller cross-reference over 374 addressable endpoints: 190 have no test, 140 have no UI caller, 91 have neither. Routers with 0 tested endpoints and their UI-wired counts: automation 0/5 ui=0, decisions 0/4 ui=3, feedback 0/3 ui=0, goals 0/5 ui=4, improvements 0/4 ui=4, journal 0/3 ui=2, learn 0/8 ui=7, learned_skills 0/5 ui=4, learning_verification 0/2 ui=0, macros 0/5 ui=5, mood 0/3 ui=1, obsidian 0/6 ui=6, onboarding 0/7 ui=7, operating_manual 0/5 ui=0, plugins 0/3 ui=0, timeline 0/4 ui=1, vision 0/2 ui=0, world_state 0/2 ui=1. Corroborated by agent/scripts/check_api_contracts.py's own run: 181 uncovered routes.
risk: Low risk to the product, high cost in maintainer time — 181 uncovered routes is not a sprint. Sequence it behind the CI ratchet so the number cannot grow while it is being paid down, and accept partial progress. Endpoint tests that spin a TestClient are already an established pattern here (the 'endpoint' pytest marker exists in pyproject.toml).

challenge.reason:
The factual core survives challenge; the prescription does not. VERIFIED: I extracted every literal URL passed to a TestClient verb across agent/tests (109 unique paths) and found zero hits on onboarding, learn, macros, obsidian, goals, improvements, decisions, or learned_skills. Running C:/Work/Programming/Layla/agent/scripts/check_api_contracts.py under .venv-test reproduces the number exactly: "181 uncovered routes exceeds threshold 125; 56 above limit... FAIL: 56 issue(s) found". The 18-router population is real and distinct from the orphans.

But three corrections change what should actually be done, and they cut the effort from weeks to hours.

(1) THE RATCHET ALREADY EXISTS AND IS ITSELF AN INSTANCE OF THE SIGNATURE DEFECT. The recommendation says to "sequence it behind the CI ratchet" as future work. check_api_contracts.py has carried MAX_UNCOVERED_ROUTES = 125 since 2026-05-13 with the comment "This number must only go DOWN." It is now at 181 — breached by 56 — and nobody noticed, because agent/scripts/run_all_checks.py registers it as severity "WARN" and ripgrep over .github/ finds no reference to run_all_checks or check_api_contracts in ci.yml, release.yml, or verify-deep.yml. A gate that was built, set, and never plugged into CI, silently drifting 45% past its own baseline, is exactly the failure mode this codebase is known for. Repairing it (reset baseline to the true 181, flip WARN→FAIL, add one CI step) is an hours-level fix and is the single highest-value action in this whole recommendation. It should be the headline, not a sequencing footnote.

(2) THE PRIORITISATION IS INVERTED. The recommendation names onboarding as the top target because it is the first-run path. AST over agent/routers/onboarding.py: 7 endpoints, median body 2 statements, MAX body 2 statements. Every one is a pure pass-through to a service. goals is median 2 / max 3; obsidian max 5; learn max 8. An HTTP test on a two-statement delegation catches route registration, method/path, and response shape — real, but shallow, and identical in nature across all 181 routes. That is a job for one parametrized sweep (iterate app.routes, GET every read-only route, assert status not in {404, 500}), which covers the large majority of the 181 in a single file in hours. Hand-writing 181 bespoke endpoint tests buys little more and costs the weeks the recommendation admits it costs.

(3) "NO REGRESSION PROTECTION WHATSOEVER" IS OVERSTATED. The probe measures HTTP-layer coverage only — check_api_contracts.py greps tests for literal fetch('/path') / client.get('/path') strings. Service layers under these routers are tested: agent/tests/test_obsidian_sync.py exercises services/infrastructure/obsidian_sync.py directly (set_vault_path, get_vault_path, md-file enumeration, hidden-dir skip); agent/tests/test_skill_acquisition.py imports services/skills/macros.py; test_first_run_tour.py and test_ui_element_contract.py touch onboarding. The uncovered surface is the thin wrapper, not the behaviour. Separately, the checker's literal-string matching cannot see dynamically built URLs — 29 f-string TestClient calls exist (test_plans_api, test_cluster_e2e, test_codex_router, test_projects_api and others), so 181 is a ceiling, not an exact count. The probe is directionally sound but should not be quoted as if it measured risk.

Net: accept the finding, reject the plan. Do the ratchet repair plus a generic route sweep in hours; hand-write bespoke tests only for the routers with real logic in the handler (learn at max 8 statements, obsidian at 5), not for the 2-statement passthroughs.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Flipping check_api_contracts.py to FAIL without first resetting MAX_UNCOVERED_ROUTES breaks CI on the next push immediately — it is 56 over its own baseline today. Reset the constant to the measured 181 in the same commit that adds the CI step, then ratchet down; do not "fix" the breach by raising the number silently, which is how it got to 125 from 118 in the first place. For the generic sweep: restrict it to GET and exclude anything side-effectful or slow — several of these routers front the model, the filesystem, or the live DB (obsidian writes a vault path, learn can trigger inference), so an indiscriminate sweep over all methods would mutate state, hit the 3B, or hang CI. Path-parameter routes ({aspect_id}, {task_id}, {conversation_id}) need fixture IDs or they will return 404 and the sweep will assert on the wrong thing — either seed them or exclude them explicitly rather than weakening the assertion to "not 500", which would let genuine routing breakage pass. Nothing is deleted and no dependency is added, so there is no product-surface risk; the only real cost is maintainer time, and the corrected plan spends hours of it instead of weeks.
