title: Reconcile [core] vs [cpu] duplication — two near-identical 20-line dependency lists drift silently
rationale: [core] and [cpu] are hand-maintained near-duplicates: [cpu] is documented as '[core] but WITHOUT chromadb', yet it also silently adds model2vec and sqlite-vec, which is exactly the divergence that makes the fast embedder path unreachable from [all]. Two parallel hand-edited lists of ~20 entries guarantee drift, and the drift is invisible because no gate compares them (the parity test explicitly scopes [cpu] out). Restructure so the shared set is declared once — e.g. a [base] extra that both [core] and [cpu] reference via layla[base], the same self-reference mechanism [all] already uses and that the parity test already knows how to expand to a fixed point. This makes the real difference between the two profiles a two-line diff that a reader can actually verify.
evidence: pyproject.toml:31-63 ([core], 21 entries) vs :69-97 ([cpu], 22 entries). Set difference: [core] has chromadb; [cpu] has model2vec and sqlite-vec; all other entries are byte-identical including the duplicated 3-line prometheus_client comment block, which appears verbatim at both :60-62 and :94-96. The parity gate already implements layla[x] self-reference expansion (_extra_to_dists, 'expand self-references (only [all] uses them today) to a fixed point'), so the mechanism exists and is tested.
risk: Moderate — restructuring extras changes what existing install commands resolve to. install/fresh_install.ps1 is documented as depending on the [cpu] profile (pyproject.toml:68 references it and REQ-72), so the installer and docs/ must be updated in lockstep or the laptop install path breaks. Verify test_dependency_parity.py still passes after the change, since it reads extras directly.
area: Dependency + API surface hygiene (pyproject extras, requirements.txt, CI pin discipline, licence posture, routers/ HTTP surface)

title: Make pip-audit blocking, or record why it is not
rationale: The dependency vulnerability audit runs but cannot fail the build — its result is swallowed into a non-blocking warning annotation. For a source-available product that ships a large third-party surface (65 declared dists, plus a torch/transformers stack), that means a known-vulnerable dependency can enter and stay indefinitely with only a warning nobody reads. This may well be a deliberate call — pip-audit is noisy and a solo maintainer cannot chase every transitive advisory — in which case the right fix is to make the choice explicit and bounded (fail on HIGH/CRITICAL only, or maintain an ignore list with dated review) rather than to leave it decorative. Note this is a smaller concern than it looks precisely because the licence gate next to it IS blocking and correct.
evidence: .github/workflows/ci.yml:50-54 — 'Dependency vulnerability audit (pip-audit)' ... 'pip-audit --skip-editable --progress-spinner off || echo "::warning::pip-audit reported advisories (non-blocking; review)"'. The `|| echo` makes the step always succeed. Contrast with ci.yml:47-48 'License compliance — no strong-copyleft deps (REQ-02) / run: python scripts/check_copyleft.py', which has no such guard and does block.
risk: Making it blocking outright will very likely turn CI red immediately on transitive advisories in the torch/transformers/chromadb stack that this project cannot fix and does not meaningfully expose (it is local-first and does not accept untrusted network input on most paths). A permanently-red gate gets bypassed. Scope to severity thresholds, or leave non-blocking and document the rationale in SECURITY.md.
area: Dependency + API surface hygiene (pyproject extras, requirements.txt, CI pin discipline, licence posture, routers/ HTTP surface)

title: Delete AgentRunRequest + autonomous_run_from_request (dead, kept alive by a test)
rationale: A 30-field dataclass and its wrapper with zero callers in prod AND zero in tests. It survives only because test_architecture_boundaries.py asserts the two NAMES exist on agent_loop — a test enforcing an API nobody calls. Delete both plus the two list entries.
evidence: AST call-graph over 522 files: autonomous_run_from_request = 0 PROD callsites, 0 test callsites. Defined agent_loop.py:479 (AgentRunRequest) and agent_loop.py:519. Only textual refs: agent/tests/test_architecture_boundaries.py:404-405 (name-existence list), plus docs/design/01-core-agent-loop.md:19 and ENGINEERING_BLUEPRINT.md:1004.
risk: None. No caller exists. The only thing that breaks is the name-assertion test, which must be edited in the same commit (remove the two strings from _REQUIRED_AGENT_LOOP_ATTRS).

challenge.reason: Verified by AST over 1475 py files (worktrees excluded, precondition-asserted): exactly 2 definitions and ZERO external references. The sole Load-ref to AgentRunRequest is the type annotation inside autonomous_run_from_request's own signature (agent_loop.py:519). No imports, no calls, no attribute access in prod or test. No dynamic surface: agent_loop.py has no __all__, no getattr(agent_loop,...) pattern exists in the package, and the names appear in no router, tool registry, or plugin/skill-pack API. Critically, the remote/clustering pillar (which the operator ruled must be WIRED, not deleted) does not need this: agent/services/cluster/work_unit.py has its own WorkUnit dataclass with payload:dict, and TaskType has no agent-run member (inference/embedding/ingestion/study/backup/consolidation/wiki_build), so cluster dispatch never routes an agent run through AgentRunRequest. Deleting removes nothing that pillar depends on. TWO CORRECTIONS to the recommendation: (1) it is 29 fields, not 30; I checked parity against the live signature and it is 29/29 with no drift in either direction, so the wrapper would actually work if called -- it is dead, not broken, a slightly weaker rot argument than implied but irrelevant to the verdict. (2) The recommendation scopes the edit at 2 files and dismisses the docs as mere 'textual refs'. The 3 doc lines must go in the SAME commit -- docs/design/01-core-agent-loop.md:19 documents it in the core-loop API table, :425 analyzes its conversation_history default, ENGINEERING_BLUEPRINT.md:1004 marks P2-5 'Done'. Leaving them manufactures exactly this codebase's signature residue: docs describing an API that does not exist. Honest scope is 4 files, still minutes. Underlying finding worth noting: _REQUIRED_AGENT_LOOP_ATTRS is labeled 'backward compat' but has no consumer to be compatible with -- a test enforcing an imagined API. The other 10 entries in that list are mostly genuinely live (autonomous_run, TOOLS, classify_intent) but the list deserves a glance on a later pass.
challenge.what_it_would_break: Nothing at runtime -- no caller exists on any code path. The only breakage is test_architecture_boundaries.py::test_agent_loop_backward_compat_attrs, which does hasattr() checks against _REQUIRED_AGENT_LOOP_ATTRS (agent/tests/test_architecture_boundaries.py:404-405); the two strings must be removed in the same commit. Secondary: if the 3 doc references (docs/design/01-core-agent-loop.md:19 and :425, ENGINEERING_BLUEPRINT.md:1004) are not updated in the same commit, the repo gains stale documentation for a nonexistent API. No user-facing surface, no HTTP route, no plugin/extensibility contract, no clustering dependency.

title: write_file / shell / fetch_url still discard the model's args — the P13-E1 bug is not closed
rationale: The fix that made tools actually run was applied to five handlers and missed three, including the single most consequential write tool. These handlers parse the goal STRING for their arguments; when parsing fails they set status=parse_failed and break WITHOUT appending a step, and agent_loop's fallback then emits confident prose. That is precisely the fabrication path the P13-E commits claim to have closed. Route all three through the same args-first helper.
evidence: AST scan of all 20 _handle_* functions in services/tools/tool_dispatch.py: _handle_write_file model_args=False, parses via al._extract_file_and_content (services/agent/intent_classifier.py:65 — requires literal substring 'with content' AND a token containing ':' or '\\' or '/'); _handle_shell model_args=False, al._extract_shell_argv (intent_classifier.py:81 — takes a quoted substring of the goal); _handle_fetch_url model_args=False, tool_dispatch.py:583 `next((w for w in goal.split() if w.startswith('http')),'')`. Contrast _arg_or_goal_path at tool_dispatch.py:205 whose own docstring documents this exact failure for read_file.
risk: write_file and shell are the two most destructive tools; a handler that starts honouring model-supplied paths will reach paths the goal-text heuristic could never name. Land it behind the existing sandbox/approval gates and add a test that a bare-filename write_file emits a tool step, not a reason step.

title: Funnel all 45 state["steps"].append sites through one record_step()
rationale: This is the highest simplification-per-risk change available. One function that appends the step AND updates last_tool_used/tool_calls/blocked_calls together makes 'status=finished with steps=[]' structurally impossible, and gives one place to instrument. It is exactly what smolagents does with ActionStep and what the OpenAI runner does when it appends tool results — one append, at loop level, after the action returns. No behaviour change on day one: make record_step a pure move.
evidence: AST: 45 `state["steps"].append(...)` sites — tool_dispatch.py 29, decision_loop.py 7, tool_guards.py 5, tool_dispatch_base.py 2, agent_loop.py 1, reasoning_handler.py 1. Status is written independently at 20 sites in 5 files, so the two facts about a turn are maintained by disjoint code.
risk: Mechanical but wide — 45 call sites across 6 files. Some appends carry extra keys (deliberated, aspect); record_step must accept **extra or the step shape silently changes and the UI step renderer drops fields. Do it as one commit, no logic edits.
area: The agent loop architecture itself — router → coordinator → autonomous_run → run_setup → decision_loop → llm_decision → tool_dispatch → run_finalizer/turn_commit → response

title: Collapse autonomous_run → _autonomous_run_impl → _autonomous_run_impl_core into one function plus one contextmanager
rationale: Three of the fourteen layers are one logical step. The outer sets contextvars + prompt optimizer + scheduling lock; the middle does NOTHING but set/clear model override, reasoning effort and tool permissions in a try/finally; the core is the real body. The middle layer is a hand-rolled decorator. One `with _run_scope(...)` contextmanager wrapping the core removes two frames and two 30-argument parameter lists that must be kept in sync by hand.
evidence: agent_loop.py:559 autonomous_run (31 params) → :687 _autonomous_run_impl (31 params, body is set_model_override/set_reasoning_effort/set_tool_permissions + try/return/finally) → :821 _autonomous_run_impl_core. The same 31-name argument list is retyped three times.
risk: Low, but the finally-blocks clear thread-local tool permissions and model overrides — a botched contextmanager leaks allow_run into the next turn. Keep the try/finally semantics identical and assert clear-on-exception in a test.

title: Drop the three dead ExecutionState fields and declare the 60 undeclared ones
rationale: The constructor allocates fields with zero consumers, while 60 keys the loop actually depends on are never declared anywhere. That asymmetry is why resume and serialization are guesswork. Delete the dead three; move the 60 runtime-grown keys into create_initial (or a TypedDict) so the state surface is inspectable in one place.
evidence: AST: ExecutionState.create_initial declares 46 keys; union of all keys touched = 106; loop modules touch 80. Zero prod consumers anywhere by any variable name for 'retries', 'memory_hits', 'last_reasoning_mode'. 'current_step' has 3 prod consumers, all in missions_db/mission_manager on a different dict — the comment at execution_state.py:87 already admits it is 'reserved'.
risk: Low for the deletions. Declaring 60 keys with defaults changes `state.get(k)` from None-by-absence to a declared default — a handful of `if 'k' in state` checks could flip. Grep for `in state` before landing.

title: Merge coordinator.dispatch_autonomous_run into coordinator.run
rationale: Two layers, one caller. dispatch_ defensively RE-BUILDS the coordinator trace because it cannot know whether its single caller already built one — a check that exists only because the boundary exists. Merging removes a layer and the redundant classification work (which on this CPU box is not free).
evidence: AST: dispatch_autonomous_run has exactly 1 prod callsite — coordinator.py:317, inside coordinator.run. coordinator.py:123-133 re-runs build_coordinator_trace when trace.get('complexity_score') is None; coordinator.py:245 already set kw['coordinator_trace'] = trace two frames up.
risk: dispatch_ also owns persistent-task create/update and the session execution snapshot; those must land inside the retry loop exactly as now or a retried turn double-creates task rows.

title: Tools must not end the turn — make DispatchResult report an outcome, let the loop decide
rationale: The single biggest structural payoff. Today a leaf tool handler writes status='finished'/'parse_failed' and returns flow='break', so the innermost frame terminates the run. That is why termination logic is unreviewable: it is spread over 30 sites at four depths. Both reference implementations forbid this — in the OpenAI Agents SDK the loop ends only on final_output, max_turns or exception; in smolagents only final_answer or max_steps. Change DispatchResult to carry `outcome` (ok / bad_args / needs_approval / refused) and have run_decision_loop own every status write and every break. Do it AFTER record_step so the append is already centralized.
evidence: AST: 13 `DispatchResult(flow='break')` sites (12 in tool_dispatch.py, 1 in tool_dispatch_base.py) and 12 `state['status']=` writes in tool_dispatch.py — 6 'finished', 6 'parse_failed'. Loop-level: 6 top-level breaks (decision_loop.py:96,101,149,475,494,534) + 3 returns + 6 early_exit returns in run_setup.py + 2 status writes in reasoning_handler.
risk: Genuinely risky and it touches the approval gate: _handle_shell sets status='finished' after writing a pending-approval record, and that break is what stops an unapproved command from proceeding. Any refactor must preserve fail-closed on the approval path — port that case first, with a test, before the other twelve.

challenge.reason: The premise is factually inverted. DispatchResult is a returned dataclass, not a control transfer: handlers do NOT terminate the run. AST + ripgrep show exactly ONE production consumer of .flow — decision_loop.py:474 — with every other hit being a test assertion. The loop already owns every break on the tool path, which is the architecture the recommendation proposes building. The "four depths" claim is wrong: mapping all 12 break sites to owning functions shows they are flat sibling handlers one level below dispatch_tool_intent (a pure _HANDLER_MAP lookup), which is one level below the loop — two frames, one decision point. The "30 sites" is inflated by conflating unrelated paths: the 6 early_exit returns in run_setup.py are pre-loop guards returning a dict before the agent loop is entered (memory answer, content guard, plan pipeline), not tool termination, and the 8 bare breaks in decision_loop.py are ordinary loop control inside the single loop function — exactly what the cited reference SDKs do. The counts quoted in the evidence are accurate (12+1 breaks, 12 status writes = 6 finished / 6 parse_failed); it is the interpretation that fails. Residual truth: handlers write state['status'] AND return flow='break', two signals to keep in sync, and folding them into one `outcome` enum is a legitimate cleanup. But the loop cannot derive 'finished' vs 'parse_failed' from flow alone, so the enum is needed regardless — the change is a mechanical rename of 13 sites plus ~32 test assertions. Hours, not days. The stated payoff ("termination logic becomes reviewable") is already achieved: one grep, one line. Days of risky refactoring through the shell approval gate for a rename is a bad trade for one maintainer.
challenge.what_it_would_break: The shell approval gate (agent/services/tools/tool_dispatch.py:601-652) has two paths that write a pending-approval record via al._write_pending, append an approval_required step, set status='finished' and return flow='break'. That break is what prevents an unapproved command from proceeding — it is currently correct and fail-closed. A refactor that re-routes the termination decision risks converting a working fail-closed path into a fail-open regression, for a benefit that amounts to renaming a field. Also touched: ~32 assertions in agent/tests/test_tool_dispatch.py and agent/tests/test_shell_approval_gate.py that assert on res.flow directly, plus the single loop consumer at agent/services/agent/decision_loop.py:474.

title: Split routers/agent.py::agent() — 1281 lines, 18 exits, 16 commit_turn calls
rationale: The real complexity center. One async function holds three whole response strategies (fast path, multi-agent, full agentic) as three inline async generators, and every one of its 18 exits must remember to call commit_turn with the right status — 16 hand-written call sites, each a chance to persist the wrong status or none. Extract the three generators into named handlers and funnel persistence through ONE exit that takes (reply, status, result). That alone removes 15 opportunities to forget.
evidence: AST on routers/agent.py (BOM-aware): agent() spans lines 347-1628 = 1281 lines; 9 nested functions ['_understand_sync','run_agent','agen','agen_fast','agen_ma','_worker','_stream_worker','_get','_tok_get']; 18 top-level returns; 16 JSONResponse/StreamingResponse constructions; 16 commit_turn callsites (19 in prod repo-wide, 3 more in openai_compat). Comment at agent.py:821 notes commit_turn is deliberately NOT self-deduplicating — so a double-call double-writes.
risk: Highest-traffic code path in the product; every streaming edge case (client disconnect, busy, timeout, pipeline_needs_input) is currently handled by its own return+commit pair. Extract mechanically, one strategy per commit, and diff the emitted SSE frames before/after.

challenge.reason: The metrics are all correct (agent() = 347-1628, 1281 lines, 9 nested funcs, 18 returns, 16 commit_turn), but the causal claim is wrong on four counts. (1) "18 exits must each remember commit_turn" is false: only 3 of the 18 top-level returns commit (677, 770, 1537); lines 471-650 are plan-mode/validation branches that must NOT commit. The other 13 commit_turn calls are inside the three generators (agen 9, agen_fast 3, agen_ma 1) and would move with them into the extracted handlers unchanged — the refactor removes zero call sites, not 15. (2) Exactly-once is ALREADY enforced: each generator opens `_committed = {"done": False}` (agent.py:825, 1074), every commit site sets it, and the except/finally paths gate on `if not _committed["done"]`. The cited double-write risk from non-deduplicating commit_turn is already closed. (3) The proposed mechanism is structurally impossible for its highest-value case: client-abort is committed in `finally` (936-952) because uvicorn throws GeneratorExit (a BaseException) into the generator on tab-close — a generator being torn down has no return value, so it cannot funnel through "ONE exit that takes (reply, status, result)". On a box where a warm turn is ~70s, that abort path is the most frequent persistence event. (4) The 16 sites are payload, not boilerplate: each pairs a distinct status (finished/blocked/error/system_busy/timeout/client_abort/pipeline_needs_input/refused) with a distinct SSE done-frame and deliberately different durable vs. context-cache text (comment at 1128-1131). A funnel still requires every branch to build (reply, status, refused, state). What IS real is duplication: lines 1132-1137, 1141-1146, 1151-1156, 1161-1166, 1172-1177 are five near-identical 5-line commit+append+append+yield blocks. Corrected recommendation: extract one helper `_terminate(reply, status, **extra) -> str` that commits, appends history, and returns the SSE frame — hours, not days, leaves finally/GeneratorExit semantics untouched, and is verifiable by diffing emitted frames. Also relevant: this is freshly-hardened BL-245 code that fixed live data loss, and ripgrep found no test file importing routers.agent or exercising the /agent route, so a days-long structural refactor of the hottest path has no safety net for one maintainer.
challenge.what_it_would_break: As proposed: the client-abort persistence path. commit_turn for GeneratorExit lives in `finally` (agent.py:936-952) and cannot return a terminal value; routing persistence through a single return-value exit would silently drop turns whenever the operator closes the tab or hits stop — the exact bug BL-245 was written to fix, and the most common one on a ~70s-per-turn CPU box. Secondarily, the per-status SSE done-frames differ in shape (questions[], artifacts[], blocked, deliberation, ux_states, reasoning_mode); collapsing them behind one funnel risks emitting frames the UI does not expect, with no test coverage of routers.agent to catch it.

title: Break the agent_loop ↔ services/agent ring left behind by the extraction
rationale: The split moved code out but kept agent_loop as the address book, so the extracted modules import back into it. decision_loop reaches _al._llm_decision, which is a wrapper that injects three registries into llm_decision.decide — dependency injection whose only purpose is to dodge the cycle the facade created. Move the handful of genuinely shared leaf helpers (_format_steps, _VALID_TOOLS, TOOLS access, _extract_*) into a dependency-free module both sides import, and let decision_loop call llm_decision directly.
evidence: 79 occurrences of `_impl` in agent_loop.py (alias assignments + thin wrappers, e.g. agent_loop.py:438 _llm_decision, :781 _run_tool_guards, :800 _finalize_run_state — each just re-passes format_steps_fn/tools_registry/valid_tools). 37 distinct `_al.<attr>` accesses from services/*. 21 prod modules import agent_loop. tool_dispatch_base.py:73 documents the workaround: a cached lazy `_imports()` returning (agent_loop, runtime_safety, TOOLS).
risk: Import-order sensitive — the lazy `_imports()` caches exist because eager imports deadlocked. Move leaves only, one module per commit, and keep test_startup_imports/test_architecture_boundaries green at each step.

challenge.reason:
The recommendation's core prescription is already implemented, and its scope is inflated ~5x. It asks to "move shared leaf helpers (_format_steps, _VALID_TOOLS, TOOLS access, _extract_*) into a dependency-free module both sides import" — that module exists: agent/services/agent/step_formatting.py exports VALID_TOOLS, format_steps, summarize_steps_deterministic and imports TOOLS directly from layla.tools.registry; _extract_* already live in services/agent/intent_classifier.py. AST check of 10 leaf modules (llm_decision, step_formatting, ux_emitter, tool_helpers, verification_engine, response_builder, reasoning_state, goal_context, intent_classifier, probe_helpers) shows ZERO imports of agent_loop — every "agent_loop" hit is the docstring "Extracted from agent_loop.py". Nothing is left to move.

The evidence is also mischaracterized. The 79 `_impl` occurrences are not cycle-dodging DI: AST shows 31 are top-level alias assignments (_emit_ux = _emit_ux_impl) and the rest ImportFrom re-exports. Of the 37 distinct `_al.<attr>` reads from services, 24 are PURE PASS-THROUGHS to sibling services.agent.* modules — services import agent_loop to reach their own siblings. Only 13 are genuinely agent_loop-owned (autonomous_run, system_overloaded, _get_effective_config, _reason_mode_lock, 4 UX_STATE_* constants, _SANITIZED_PLACEHOLDER, 3 DI wrappers).

The DI claim is mechanically wrong. services/agent/llm_decision.py imports no agent_loop, so it has no cycle to dodge. Its format_steps_fn/tools_registry/valid_tools_all are kwonly with None defaults and NO internal fallback (line 214 calls format_steps_fn directly) — None is a TypeError trap, not optional injection. The DI is redundant but for a different reason than stated. Also imprecise: the function is llm_decision(), not llm_decision.decide().

The real remaining work is narrow and cheap: redirect 23 pass-through call sites in decision_loop.py, reasoning_handler.py, run_setup.py, tool_dispatch_base.py and multi_agent.py to direct sibling imports, then delete the dead _imports() cache at tool_dispatch_base.py:73. No code movement, no new module, no import-order risk. That is SIMPLIFY at hours scale, not CONSOLIDATE at days scale.

Judged against one maintainer on a CPU-only box: the residual ring is aesthetic, not a wiring defect (agent_loop is the legitimate public entry point — 12 of its importers just want autonomous_run). This is the same code family that only just got tool-calling working in P13-E after 16 days of zero tool execution; a days-long refactor of the hottest path for tidiness is the wrong trade.
challenge.what_it_would_break:
The unpriced cost is the test seam. `_al.X` is late-bound module attribute lookup, which is the interception point the suite depends on: 85 direct monkeypatch.setattr sites on agent_loop attrs across 129 test files — _llm_decision at 12 sites, system_overloaded 16, autonomous_run 9, _get_effective_config 4 (41 sites total on attrs services reach via _al.). The recommendation's explicit ask — "let decision_loop call llm_decision directly" — silently severs all 12 monkeypatch.setattr(agent_loop, "_llm_decision", ...) sites: tests stop controlling the code path while still asserting on outputs. The proposed green-gate (test_startup_imports, test_architecture_boundaries) is AST/filesystem-only and would not catch this.

The mitigating fact that redefines the safe scope: of the 24 pass-through attrs, 23 have ZERO overlap with the monkeypatch seam (only run_completion collides). So the hours-scale redirect is safe and mechanically verifiable, while the three DI wrappers (_llm_decision, _run_tool_guards, _run_verification_after_tool) and the agent_loop-owned attrs must KEEP their late-bound seam. Doing the full refactor as written would churn the agent's hottest path — the one just stabilized in P13-E — and risk regressing tool dispatch for no user-visible gain.

title: Crash-resume restores 8 of 106 state keys, and one of the 8 is dead
rationale: resume_execution_state is the crash-recovery contract, and it is an 8-name hardcoded tuple against a 106-key live surface. Everything else — packed_context, policy_caps, no_op_steps, blocked_calls, tool_loop_history, used_learning_ids — resets. A resumed run therefore re-earns budget it already spent and can re-run guarded tools. Decide deliberately what resume means (probably: steps + all counters), rather than inheriting whichever eight names someone typed. Classified INVESTIGATE because the right answer depends on whether resume is meant to be exact or best-effort — that is the operator's call, not mine.
evidence: services/agent/run_setup.py:418-420 — _rk = ('depth','tool_calls','consecutive_no_progress','last_tool_used','strategy_shift_count','status','pipeline_stage','retries'). AST: 'retries' has 0 prod consumers anywhere by any variable name, so 7 of 8 do work. Live key surface = 106 (46 declared + 60 runtime-grown). Note 'blocked_calls', the backstop counter, is NOT restored.
risk: Restoring more counters makes resumed runs terminate sooner — correct, but it will change the behaviour of any test that resumes and expects a full budget. Cheap to scope: the fix is a list, the decision is the work.

title: Do NOT adopt LangGraph, and do not restructure toward a graph runtime
rationale: Standing-duty research answer, recorded so it is not re-litigated. The loop here is already ReAct with a depth cap of 5 and essentially one branch (tool vs reason). LangGraph's value — branching, durable checkpointing, concurrent reducers — buys nothing at that scale and costs a graph compiler, channel/reducer semantics and a checkpointer for one maintainer to own; published guidance says a direct call is simpler and faster below ~3 steps with no branching, and its default state reducers are a documented token-leak footgun in long-running loops. What IS worth copying, dependency-free, is the SHAPE of smolagents and the OpenAI Agents SDK: typed step records appended at exactly one place, and only three termination conditions with the model owning the answer. Those are recommendations 3 and 7 above and require no new package.
evidence: Research: smolagents MultiStepAgent — memory is SystemPromptStep/TaskStep/ActionStep, one ActionStep appended per iteration at loop level, loop ends on final_answer or max_steps (huggingface.co/docs/smolagents conceptual_guides/react). OpenAI Agents SDK Runner — exactly three termination conditions: final_output, MaxTurnsExceeded, exception; tool results are appended and the loop re-runs, a tool never terminates (openai.github.io/openai-agents-python/running_agents). Measured local counterpart: 48 turn-ending sites, 45 append sites.
risk: None — this is a decision to not act. The risk is the opposite: treating 'adopt a framework' as the fix for accretion that is actually concentrated in tool_dispatch and routers/agent.py.
oss_candidate: Evaluated and REJECTED as dependencies: LangGraph (graph compiler + checkpointer + reducer semantics; MIT; very actively maintained; overkill for a linear 5-iteration loop, and cloud/LangSmith gravity conflicts with local-first), smolagents (Apache-2.0, actively maintained, genuinely small — but swapping the loop is a rewrite, which is off the table, and its CodeAgent execution model is wrong for a 3B on CPU). ADOPT THE PATTERNS, NOT THE PACKAGES: single-append typed step record, and model-only turn termination. Both are internal refactors with zero new supply chain.

title: Fix the UTF-8 BOM on routers/agent.py — it silently excludes the file from AST tooling
rationale: routers/agent.py starts with U+FEFF, so ast.parse() with plain utf-8 raises SyntaxError. Any repo-wide static analysis that skips unparseable files silently omits the single most important file in the turn path — 1281 lines of it. My own first probe did exactly this and I only caught it because the probe printed its skip count. Strip the BOM.
evidence: ast.parse(open(p,encoding='utf-8')) on agent/routers/agent.py → SyntaxError: invalid non-printable character U+FEFF at line 1. First probe run reported 'PARSED FILES: 521 SKIPPED: 1'; after switching to utf-8-sig, 522/0.
risk: None to Python (the interpreter accepts a BOM). Only risk is a noisy one-line diff and a merge conflict if the file is being edited concurrently.
